Key findings: (1) text communication causes significant homogenization (CAF=0.803 [0.740, 0.873], d=1.30, p<0.001), confirmed by no-communication ablation and prompt-perturbation controls; (2) image communication also homogenizes under within-modality baselines (CAF=0.834 [0.811, 0.858]), with comparable proportional effect; (3) group size moderates coupling direction -- K=5 produces homogenization while K=3 yields CAF > 1.0 (point estimates 1.14 and 1.06, CI pending), suggesting a directional shift toward diversification; (4) cross-model replication shows extreme variation (CAF 0.034-0.803), with DeepSeek dominated by format artifacts; (5) coupling is stateless -- driven by prompt context rather than cumulative updating, with continuous consensus producing monotonic convergence. These results establish LLM agent coupling as real, measurable, and controllable at the prompt level, with direct implications for multi-agent system design.

AI.tldr: The study investigates how large language models (LLMs) communicate as agents, using a protocol called BOUNDARY_SYNC to measure whether inter-agent communication leads to homogenization or diversification of outputs. Key findings show significant homogenization in both text and image communications.
AI.motivation: As LLMs increasingly function as communicating agents, understanding how their interactions impact outputs can guide better designs of multi-agent systems.
AI.method: The authors used a BOUNDARY_SYNC protocol involving controlled GPT-4o experiments with ~9,900 API calls to measure coupling amplification in text and image communications, analyzing the effects of group size and cross-model replication.
AI.result: Text communication caused significant homogenization (CAF=0.803), image communication showed a similar trend (CAF=0.834), group size influenced directional shifts, and coupling was driven by prompt context rather than cumulative updating.
AI.conclusion: LLM agent coupling is measurable and can be controlled via prompt modifications, suggesting immediate implications for optimizing multi-agent system designs.

title: WorldSample: Closed-loop Real-robot RL with World Modelling
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) can overcome the demonstration-coverage limitation of imitation learning (IL) by allowing robots to improve through trial-and-error interaction beyond the states observed in demonstrations. However, deploying RL on real robots remains constrained by high interaction costs, since each physical rollout is costly and reflects only one realized action-outcome path. To address this challenge, we propose WorldSample, a physically grounded data augmentation framework for real-robot RL that closes a real-synthetic loop between physical rollouts, world-model generation, and policy improvement. Grounded on real rollouts, WorldSample generates high-fidelity synthetic transitions through a post-trained world model, which greatly lowers the visual hallucination. Specifically, rather than simply using these transitions as real-world experience, WorldSample introduces Policy-Paced Learning (PPL) to regulate the training process through sample selection and scheduling, balancing useful augmentation against value overestimation and mitigating the hallucination-induced noise. Experiments on robot manipulation tasks involving contact-rich and precise tasks show that WorldSample improves policy success rate by 28% while reducing training steps by 59% compared with baselines. Furthermore, WorldSample improves world model visual fidelity by 19.4dB in PSNR and 0.47 in SSIM over demonstration-only post-training, validating the effectiveness of the real-synthetic loop for both policy and world model performance.

AI.tldr: WorldSample is a data augmentation technique for real-world robot reinforcement learning (RL), addressing high costs of physical rollouts by creating accurate synthetic data to improve learning efficiency and success rates.
AI.motivation: Traditional RL for robots is limited by the high cost and inefficiency of physical rollouts. The goal is to enable robots to learn efficiently, beyond imitation learning, in real-world scenarios without incurring excessive resource expense.
AI.method: WorldSample combines real-world robot rollouts with synthetic data generated through a post-trained world model. It regulates training using Policy-Paced Learning (PPL), which carefully selects and schedules augmented samples to balance learning and mitigate noise.
AI.result: When tested on contact-rich robot manipulation tasks, WorldSample improved the policy success rate by 28% and reduced training steps by 59%. Additionally, it enhanced world model visual fidelity by 19.4 dB (PSNR) and 0.47 (SSIM) compared to a demonstration-only scenario.
AI.conclusion: WorldSample effectively lowers the cost of deploying RL in real robots by creating a feedback loop between real and synthetic data, improving both the learning process and the quality of the world model.

title: NAVER LABS Europe Submission to the Instruction-following 2026 Short Track
summary: In this paper, we describe NAVER LABS Europe's submission to the instruction-following speech processing short track at IWSLT 2026. We participate again in the constrained setting, developing systems capable of jointly performing ASR, ST, and SQA from English speech into Chinese, Italian, and German. Building on our previous submission, ranked first in last year's short track, we update our multi-stage training pipeline by replacing the speech projector with SpeechMapper, a method for learning a speech-to-LLM embedding projector using only ASR data. In addition, we introduce a synthetic SQA dataset, fakACL, composed of artificially generated scientific presentations. This dataset is built by prompting the LLM backbone, segmenting the generated talks, and synthesizing speech with SeamlessM4T-large-v2. The combination of an improved speech projection mechanism and domain-specific synthetic data allows our model to outperform last year's best short-track system, while being considerably more compact and relying on a weaker LLM backbone. This year's results place our system tied for first place in the overall short track ranking.

AI.tldr: NAVER LABS Europe improved their system for jointly performing ASR, ST, and SQA tasks, achieving tied first place in IWSLT 2026's short-track ranking.
AI.motivation: To enhance instruction-following speech processing systems, aiming for improved performance and compactness while relying on weaker backbones.
AI.method: The paper introduces SpeechMapper for embedding projection and fakACL, a synthetic dataset with LLM-prompted scientific presentations and SeamlessM4T speech synthesis.
AI.result: The system outperformed previous year's first-place result and achieved a tie for first position in the IWSLT 2026 ranking.
AI.conclusion: Innovative embedding methods and synthetic domain-specific data enhance performance, solidifying competitive standing.

title: Developers' Experience with Generative AI Beyond Productivity Assessment -- Insights from an Empirical Mixed-Methods Field Study
summary: With the growing adoption of AI-powered coding assistants, organizations and developers are increasingly seeking to optimize their interaction with these tools. Prior research has largely focused on output quality and productivity gains, with limited attention paid to developers' well-being and interaction experiences. This paper presents a developer-centered empirical mixed-methods study to investigate how professional developers engage with Generative AI (GenAI) in their natural work environment. Controlled data collection sessions are combined with natural work periods. Results show that developers are generally satisfied with GenAI, particularly for monotonous, repetitive, and structured tasks, and report perceived efficiency and productivity gains. Copilot interaction type preferences differ by task type and complexity: While both in-code suggestions and chat-based prompting independently improve task efficiency and reduce perceived workload, combining these interaction types within a single task diminishes benefits. We propose a rule-of-thumb for selecting an interaction type based on task characteristics. During development-heavy tasks, results indicate that perceived cognitive load arises from AI interaction, while perceived productivity depends on AI output quality. Participation in this study positively influenced developers' awareness and intentional use of GenAI tools. These findings demonstrate the value of real-world, mixed-methods study designs to understand GenAI tools and developers' experiences with them.

AI.tldr: This study explores developers' interactions with AI coding assistants, finding satisfaction in structured tasks but differing preferences in interaction types depending on task complexity.
AI.motivation: To improve the understanding of developers' experiences and well-being when using AI-powered coding assistants in real-world settings.
AI.method: Combined controlled data collection and natural work periods to perform a mixed-methods analysis of developers' engagement with Generative AI tools.
AI.result: Developers favor GenAI for repetitive tasks and report increased efficiency, but combining in-code suggestions and chat-based prompts reduces task benefits.
AI.conclusion: Real-world mixed-method studies are critical to tailoring AI tools for developers, providing insights on interaction preferences and cognitive load.

title: Meta-Benchmarks for Financial-Services LLM Evaluation
summary: Public LLM leaderboards optimise for global average performance and do not capture the specific cognitive demands of financial-services work: a model that leads on MMLU-Pro may underperform on document-grounded compliance reasoning, and a coding leader may handle multi-turn customer interactions poorly. We present a meta-benchmarking framework that organises 452 publicly reported benchmarks into 41 O*NET Generalized Work Activities and aggregates those into 38 BIAN banking business domains spanning sales, operations, risk, and support work. A multiplicative weighting scheme (discrimination x coverage x recency), computed over a rolling model window, rewards benchmarks that still separate the best models, are widely reported, and remain in active use, suppressing saturated legacy tests automatically. These weights scale the K-factor in a pairwise Elo tournament, producing cross-benchmark-comparable work-activity scores without raw score normalisation; business-domain scores are weighted averages of the constituent work-activity Elos. We demonstrate the framework on a point-in-time public snapshot covering 288 models across 25 organisations as of June 2026, and describe the methodology, full taxonomy, design decisions, and limitations with the aim of making the approach reproducible for institutions facing similar selection and governance challenges.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a meta-benchmarking framework that evaluates 452 public benchmarks across O*NET work activities and banking business domains, proposing a novel weighting and scoring system tailored for financial services.
AI.motivation: Public LLM leaderboards fail to address the specific needs of financial-services work, such as compliance or multi-turn customer communication, focusing only on global average performance.
AI.method: The framework maps benchmarks to O*NET work activities and BIAN banking domains, applies a multiplicative weighting scheme to prioritize relevant and active benchmarks, and calculates scores using a pairwise Elo tournament system.
AI.result: A validated framework is demonstrated on a dataset of 288 LLMs from 25 organizations, producing cross-benchmark scores for banking-oriented evaluations.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework offers a reproducible, domain-specific benchmarking system for financial institutions, addressing both cognitive and business demands effectively.

title: ICDepth: Taming Video Diffusion Models for Video Depth Estimation via In-Context Conditioning
comment: Accepted to ECCV 2026. Project page: https://xuanhuahe.github.io/ICDepth/
summary: Monocular video depth estimation requires temporal consistency, geometric accuracy, and generalization across diverse scenarios, yet existing methods struggle to achieve all three simultaneously. Discriminative models excel at per-frame accuracy but suffer from temporal drift due to limited context windows, while generative methods improve consistency and generalization at the cost of extensive training data (10M+ samples) and lack of geometric precision. In response to these issues, we introduce \textbf{ICDepth}, a framework that adapts pre-trained text-to-video diffusion transformers for video depth estimation via In-Context Conditioning (ICC), leveraging their rich spatial-temporal priors. To address key challenges in transferring ICC from generation to dense prediction, we propose: (1)~\textbf{SAND-Attention}, which ensures precise spatial-temporal alignment via shared RoPE and enforces unidirectional attention to prevent noise contamination; (2)~\textbf{SRFM}, which injects DINOv2 semantic and resolution priors to enhance geometric precision. ICDepth achieves state-of-the-art results on multiple benchmarks with remarkable data efficiency, trained on only 0.8M frames ($6$--$13\times$ less than competing generative methods), while demonstrating strong zero-shot generalization to diverse domains.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes ICDepth, a model leveraging pre-trained text-to-video diffusion transformers for video depth estimation, achieving state-of-the-art results with less data and improved generalization.
AI.motivation: Achieve simultaneous temporal consistency, geometric accuracy, and generalization in monocular video depth estimation, challenging existing methods that are limited in one or more of these aspects.
AI.method: Introduced ICDepth using In-Context Conditioning to adapt pre-trained diffusion transformers for video depth tasks, along with SAND-Attention for alignment and SRFM for enhancing geometric precision.
AI.result: ICDepth attained state-of-the-art performance on various benchmarks, was trained on significantly less data compared to competitors, and showed strong zero-shot generalization.
AI.conclusion: ICDepth offers a novel and efficient approach to monocular video depth estimation, addressing limitations of both discriminative and generative methods and setting new benchmarks in generalization and data efficiency.

title: SINA: A Fully Automated Circuit Schematic Image to Netlist Generator Using Artificial Intelligence
summary: Recent advances in Artificial Intelligence (AI) have revolutionized Electronic Design Automation (EDA), particularly through Large Language Models (LLMs) for circuit design tasks. However, their application to analog and mixed-signal domains remains limited by the lack of machine-readable representations of existing circuit design knowledge. Circuit schematic images found in research manuscripts, textbooks, and websites constitute a vast repository of validated designs; however, these visual representations cannot be directly processed by EDA tools. Converting them into machine-readable netlists is essential for enabling simulation, verification, and building comprehensive databases for AI-based models. Current conversion methods lack generalization across both Integrated Circuit (IC) and Printed Circuit Board (PCB) level schematics. Moreover, they struggle with component recognition and connectivity inference, and fail to distinguish between connected junctions and crossing wires. In this paper, we propose SINA, an open-source circuit schematic image-to-netlist generator. SINA is a fully automated pipeline that integrates deep learning for robust component detection, connected-component labeling for accurate connectivity inference, Optical Character Recognition (OCR) for component reference designator extraction, and a Vision-Language Model (VLM) for reliable reference designator assignment. SINA handles both IC- and PCB-level schematics and incorporates dedicated crossing-wires detection to differentiate wire intersections from connections. We validate the correctness of the generated netlists using graph isomorphism techniques. Our experiments demonstrate an overall netlist generation accuracy of 96.67%, which is 2.72x higher compared to state-of-the-art approaches.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces SINA, an open-source tool for converting circuit schematic images into machine-readable netlists with a 96.67% accuracy, using advanced deep learning and vision-language techniques.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of converting circuit schematic images into machine-readable netlists, enabling their use in EDA tools and AI-based models for analog and mixed-signal circuit design.
AI.method: SINA employs a combination of deep learning for component detection, connected-component labeling for connectivity inference, OCR for extracting component reference designators, and a Vision-Language Model for assigning reference designators. It handles both IC and PCB schematics and distinguishes between connected junctions and crossing wires.
AI.result: SINA achieves a netlist generation accuracy of 96.67%, outperforming state-of-the-art methods by a factor of 2.72x.
AI.conclusion: SINA significantly advances the automation of circuit schematic image processing, providing robust, accurate, and generalizable netlist generation across various circuit types.

title: Learning to Move Before Learning to Do: Task-Agnostic pretraining for VLAs
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are fundamentally bottlenecked by the scarcity of expert demonstrations -- triplets of observations, instructions, and actions that are costly to collect at scale. We argue that this bottleneck stems from conflating two distinct learning objectives: acquiring physical competence (how to move) and acquiring semantic alignment (what to do). Crucially, only the latter requires language supervision. Building on this Decomposition Hypothesis, we propose Task-Agnostic Pretraining (TAP), a two-stage framework that first learns transferable motor priors from cheap, unlabeled interaction data -- including discarded off-task trajectories and autonomous robot play -- via a self-supervised Inverse Dynamics objective. A lightweight second stage then grounds these priors in language using minimal expert data. On the SIMPLER benchmark, TAP matches models trained on over 1M expert trajectories while using orders of magnitude less labeled data, yielding a 10% absolute gain over standard behavior cloning. On a real-world WidowX platform, TAP retains 25% success under camera perturbations where internet-scale baselines collapse to 0%, demonstrating that task-agnostic pretraining produces robust, transferable physical representations and offers a scalable path forward for Embodied AI.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a two-stage framework called Task-Agnostic Pretraining (TAP) to train Vision-Language-Action models using less expert-labeled data. This approach decouples the learning of motor skills from linguistic alignment.
AI.motivation: The study is driven by the challenge of limited expert demonstration data for Vision-Language-Action models, which hinders scalability and performance.
AI.method: The proposed TAP framework consists of two stages: (1) learning motor priors through self-supervised training on unlabeled interaction data via an Inverse Dynamics objective, and (2) grounding these priors in language with minimal expert supervision.
AI.result: TAP allows models to match performance achieved with over 1M labeled expert trajectories on the SIMPLER benchmark while using significantly less labeled data. It also maintains 25% success on a real-world robotic platform under camera perturbations, whereas baseline approaches fail.
AI.conclusion: TAP showcases the potential of task-agnostic pretraining to create robust, scalable Vision-Language-Action models by efficiently decoupling and addressing physical skill learning and linguistic grounding.

title: Beyond Supervised Clarification: Input Rewriting with LLMs for Dialogue Discourse Parsing
summary: Rewriting inputs to improve frozen downstream models has become a common strategy in modern NLP pipelines. Prior work on incremental dialogue discourse parsing (DDP) shows that supervised clarification models can rewrite fragmentary or underspecified utterances, such as resolving ellipsis or references, to improve parsing accuracy. In this work, we revisit this idea under realistic deployment conditions, where no clarification supervision is available and the clarifier must rely on zero-shot prompting or feedback from a frozen parser. Across three Segmented Discourse Representation Theory (SDRT) datasets and multiple parsers, we find that last-utterance clarification is far less reliable than suggested by supervised settings. Parser-agnostic rewriting often introduces more regressions than repairs, as edits that enable fixes also disrupt discourse cues relied upon by the parser. A best-of-8 rewriting analysis further reveals a practical ceiling: a large fraction of errors are not repairable through input rewriting alone. A parser-aware clarifier trained with GRPO reduces regressions by up to 37% by learning conservative abstention, yet still fails to produce selectivity-aware clarifications that consistently improve parsing. Together, these findings recast clarification as a selective intervention problem. We identify rewritability prediction, deciding whether an utterance is repairable before intervention, as the key missing capability for input-side optimization of frozen discourse parsers, and a critical direction for improving agentic pipelines more broadly.

AI.tldr: Clarifying underspecified inputs to improve frozen discourse parsers is unreliable without supervision. A parser-aware model reduces some issues, but selective and predictive strategies are vital for better results.
AI.motivation: This paper aims to evaluate the practicality of rewriting underspecified or fragmentary inputs to improve frozen discourse parser accuracy, particularly in realistic scenarios without explicit clarification supervision.
AI.method: The study analyzes clarification strategies like zero-shot prompting and parser feedback across discourse datasets, and tests a parser-aware clarifier trained with GRPO to improve upon parser-agnostic methods.
AI.result: Parser-agnostic rewriting often worsened performance, and even using a parser-aware clarifier improved accuracy only partially by reducing some regressions, but selectivity-aware clarifications remained elusive.
AI.conclusion: Effective clarification involves predicting when input is repairable and abstaining when it is not. This rewritability prediction is critical for input-side optimization and scalable language processing pipelines.

title: SkillFuzz: Fuzzing Skill Composition for Implicit Intents Discovery in Open Skill Marketplaces
summary: Large Language Model (LLM)-based agents increasingly automate software engineering tasks through reusable skills, natural-language instruction documents that guide planning and execution. Open skill marketplaces enable users to assemble agents by co-activating community-contributed skills, but marketplace operators typically audit skills in isolation. As a result, individually benign skills may interact to redirect an agent toward unintended objectives, which we term implicit intents. Detecting such intents is challenging because the effect emerges only through skill composition, execution environments are often unavailable at admission time, and the space of possible co-activations grows exponentially with marketplace size. In this paper, we formulate implicit-intent discovery as a fuzzing problem over skill compositions, where skill compositions are the unit under test, planning artifacts expose agent intent before execution, and deviations from a skill-free baseline serve as a differential oracle. Based on this formulation, we propose skillfuzz, the first execution-free testing approach that extracts structured skill contracts and uses contract-guided Monte Carlo Tree Search to prioritize potentially conflicting compositions. Across representative skill-marketplace workloads, skillfuzz discovers over 1,000 distinct implicit intents under a fixed query budget, confirms more than 80% of the highest-risk flagged compositions during execution-time validation, and identifies substantially more high-severity implicit intents than alternative search strategies while exploring only a fraction of the pairwise interaction space they require.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces "skillfuzz," an execution-free testing tool for detecting implicit intents in large language model (LLM)-based agents that use community-contributed skills in open skill marketplaces, addressing risks from unintentional interactions between benign skills.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the challenge of detecting unintended objectives (implicit intents) that arise from the interaction of individually harmless skills in marketplace-assembled AI agents, which are difficult to detect during isolated skill audits.
AI.method: The authors formulate implicit-intent discovery as a fuzzing problem. They propose skillfuzz, which employs techniques like structured skill contracts and contract-guided Monte Carlo Tree Search to prioritize and identify skill compositions with hidden conflicts, all without requiring execution environments.
AI.result: Skillfuzz discovered over 1,000 implicit intents using a fixed query budget and validated over 80% of high-risk flagged compositions during testing. It outperforms alternative strategies by identifying more high-severity issues while significantly reducing the interaction space explored.
AI.conclusion: The proposed skillfuzz approach provides a scalable, execution-free solution for uncovering implicit intents in LLM-based agents using skill marketplaces, offering an effective balance of accuracy and efficiency in detecting high-risk compositions.

title: Path-level Hindsight Instructions for Semantic Exploration in Vision-Language Navigation
summary: On-policy exploration is a crucial component for training robust Vision-Language Navigation agents, as it exposes the policy to a broader state distribution. However, such exploration inevitably leads to trajectories that deviate from expert demonstrations, resulting in a semantic mismatch between the executed visual stream and the original language instruction. In this work, we address this challenge by introducing Phi-Nav, a unified on-policy framework that leverages hindsight reasoning to align instructions with the agent's actual exploratory journey. Specifically, Phi-Nav operates through a three-stage dual-supervision cycle: 1) the agent performs oracle-guided on-policy exploration, sampling a trajectory while learning from expert action feedback, 2) a hindsight speaker synthesizes a path-level hindsight instruction grounded in the collected visual observations, and 3) the agent conducts a second imitation pass, treating the synthesized trajectory-instruction pair as an additional expert demonstration. Through this process, Phi-Nav bridges the critical semantic supervision gap inherent in on-policy methods, transforming semantically unlabeled movement into dense training signals. Evaluations on the R2R-CE and RxR-CE benchmarks show that Phi-Nav yields competitive performance while requiring only a fraction of the expert demonstrations used by current baselines. These results underscore the necessity of semantic exploration in VLN, positioning Phi-Nav as an effective solution for training embodied agents with limited data.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Phi-Nav, an on-policy framework for Vision-Language Navigation that bridges semantic gaps in exploration using hindsight reasoning, achieving competitive results with less reliance on expert demonstrations.
AI.motivation: The work is motivated by the challenge of semantic mismatches in Vision-Language Navigation agents during on-policy exploration, which leads to deviations from expert demonstrations.
AI.method: Phi-Nav employs a three-stage dual-supervision cycle: oracle-guided exploration with expert feedback, hindsight instruction synthesis, and a second training pass using synthesized trajectory-instruction pairs as demonstrations.
AI.result: The method achieves competitive results on the R2R-CE and RxR-CE benchmarks while using significantly fewer expert demonstrations than baselines.
AI.conclusion: Phi-Nav demonstrates that semantic exploration is necessary and effective for training embodied Vision-Language Navigation agents, particularly under data-limited conditions.

title: Structure-Aware Gaussian Splatting for Large-Scale Scene Reconstruction
summary: 3D Gaussian Splatting has demonstrated remarkable potential in novel view synthesis. In contrast to small-scale scenes, large-scale scenes inevitably contain sparsely observed regions with excessively sparse initial points. In this case, supervising Gaussians initialized from low-frequency sparse points with high-frequency images often induces uncontrolled densification and redundant primitives, degrading both efficiency and quality. Intuitively, this issue can be mitigated with scheduling strategies, which can be categorized into two paradigms: modulating target signal frequency via densification and modulating sampling frequency via image resolution. However, previous scheduling strategies are primarily hardcoded, failing to perceive the convergence behavior of scene frequency. To address this, we reframe the scene reconstruction problem from the perspective of signal structure recovery and propose SIG, a novel scheduler that synchronizes image supervision with Gaussian frequencies. Specifically, we derive the average sampling frequency and bandwidth of 3D representations, and then regulate the training image resolution and the Gaussian densification process based on scene frequency convergence. Furthermore, we introduce Sphere-Constrained Gaussians, which leverage the spatial prior of initialized point clouds to control Gaussian optimization. Our framework enables frequency-consistent, geometry-aware, and floater-free training, achieving state-of-the-art performance by a substantial margin in both efficiency and rendering quality in large-scale scenes. The code is available at: https://github.com/weiyixue999/Signal_Structure_Aware_Gaussian

AI.tldr: The paper addresses inefficiencies and quality issues in 3D Gaussian Splatting for large-scale scenes by introducing SIG, a scheduler that synchronizes image supervision with Gaussian frequencies. This improves reconstruction efficiency and rendering quality.
AI.motivation: To solve issues with uncontrolled densification and redundant primitives in large-scale 3D scene reconstruction due to mismatched image and Gaussian frequencies during training.
AI.method: The authors propose SIG, a scheduling system that regulates image resolution and Gaussian densification based on scene frequency convergence, alongside Sphere-Constrained Gaussians for better optimization.
AI.result: SIG ensures frequency-consistent, geometry-aware training and avoids redundant primitives, achieving substantial improvements in efficiency and rendering quality for large-scale scenes.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers a frequency-aware, optimized approach to novel view synthesis for large-scale 3D scenes, setting a new benchmark in both rendering efficiency and quality.

title: MKGR: Multimodal Knowledge-Graph Representation Learning for Cold-Start Protein-Protein Interaction Prediction
summary: Accurate protein-protein interaction (PPI) prediction is central to functional genomics, disease mechanism discovery, and drug development. A difficult setting arises when candidate interactions include proteins that have no observed PPI edges during training, where models relying on network topology alone often lose useful context. This paper presents \method, a multimodal representation framework for cold-start PPI prediction. \method\ combines region-aware protein sequence encoding with four protein-centered biomedical knowledge graphs, including protein-drug, protein-disease, protein-miRNA, and protein-lncRNA associations. The sequence branch extracts contextual representations from structurally informed sequence regions, while graph attention encoders learn modality-specific protein embeddings from sparse biomedical associations. A bridge reconstruction objective regularizes graph learning by recovering shared protein-entity associations, and a pair-level gating module adaptively integrates sequence and graph evidence for each candidate protein pair. Experiments on two benchmark datasets under novel-old and novel-novel cold-start settings show that \method\ consistently outperforms competitive sequence, network, and knowledge-graph baselines across ACC, F1, AUC, AUPR, and MCC.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a multimodal framework for protein-protein interaction (PPI) prediction, focusing on scenarios where proteins lack prior interaction data, and achieves superior results across multiple performance metrics.
AI.motivation: The study addresses the challenge of predicting PPIs in cases where candidate proteins lack observed interaction data during training, which is critical for advancing fields like genomics, disease research, and drug discovery.
AI.method: The proposed framework combines protein sequence encoding and biomedical knowledge graphs, using graph attention encoders and a pair-level gating module to integrate evidence from sequences and graphs, with a bridge reconstruction objective to enhance learning.
AI.result: The framework outperforms existing approaches in predicting PPIs in cold-start conditions on two benchmark datasets, achieving higher scores across metrics like ACC, F1, AUC, AUPR, and MCC.
AI.conclusion: The method effectively handles cold-start PPI prediction by leveraging multimodal data and demonstrates significant improvements over traditional models, making it a robust approach for real-world applications.

title: Learning Agile Intruder Interception using Differentiable Quadrotor Dynamics
summary: This paper presents a methodology for learning a control policy to intercept an intruder using the 3D direction unit vector to the intruder and the interceptor state. Prior deep reinforcement learning approaches assume either relative position or distance to the intruder is available, but this information is not readily accessible in real-world applications that employ passive, monocular camera sensors. Instead, we propose a solution that leverages an analytical policy gradient method using differentiable quadrotor dynamics to learn agile interception at speeds up to 10 m/s. The proposed approach outperforms baseline methods that utilize simplified point mass dynamics by an average of 30%.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a control policy methodology for intercepting intruders using 3D unit vector direction without requiring relative position or distance data.
AI.motivation: To develop an effective interception strategy that does not rely on hard-to-acquire intruder position or distance data in real-world scenarios using passive monocular cameras.
AI.method: An analytical policy gradient method coupled with differentiable quadrotor dynamics is utilized to achieve agile interception.
AI.result: The proposed approach achieves speeds up to 10 m/s and outperforms simpler point mass dynamics methods by an average of 30%.
AI.conclusion: The methodology effectively streamlines interception using real-world sensor limitations and demonstrates significant performance improvements.

title: Towards a Phonology-Informed Evaluation of Multilingual TTS
summary: Neural TTS systems can sound natural across languages, but naturalness does not guarantee the preservation of sound contrasts that distinguish words from their grammatical forms. Standard metrics like MOS do not test for this. We propose a classifier-based framework that audits TTS output against language-specific phonological patterns using human speech as a benchmark. Testing Assamese advanced tongue root (ATR) vowel harmony with Meta's MMS TTS, we show that a classifier trained on human speech transfers to synthesized speech with minimal loss. The faithfulness audit reveals that [+ATR] mid vowels are realized as [-ATR] in 1/3 tokens despite an underlying [+ATR] specification, a bias absent in human speech. At the word level, predicted ATR labels classify harmony more accurately than transcription labels, indicating a gap between intended and produced phonology. The framework offers task-specific diagnostics and generalizes to other phonological contrasts with measurable acoustic cues.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a framework to audit Neural Text-to-Speech (TTS) systems for faithfulness to language-specific phonological patterns, showing its efficacy with Assamese ATR vowel harmony.
AI.motivation: Text-to-Speech systems often prioritize naturalness but may neglect accurate preservation of phonological contrasts critical for linguistic meaning. Current evaluation metrics like MOS do not address this issue.
AI.method: A classifier-based framework compares synthesized speech against human benchmarks to audit TTS systems' compliance with language-specific phonological rules, using Meta's MMS TTS and Assamese ATR harmony as a test case.
AI.result: The classifier trained on human speech effectively audits TTS outputs, revealing that synthesized speech fails to faithfully reproduce ATR vowel harmony, with biases not observed in human speech.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework highlights phonological gaps in synthetic speech and serves as a generalizable tool for evaluating linguistic faithfulness in TTS systems.

title: Understanding Agent-Based Patching of Compiler Missed Optimizations
summary: Compiler missed optimizations refer to cases in which compilers failed to optimize certain code. It takes many compiler developers' efforts to implement or patch such missed optimizations. In this paper, we present a systematic study of how well agents patch compiler missed optimizations. We identify a significant challenge that patching a missed optimization requires more than just fixing the reported case, and instead requires generalizing to similar cases. We construct a benchmark of real-world LLVM missed optimization issues and compare agent-generated patches with patches from developers in terms of optimization scope. Our results show that coding agents often optimize the given examples, but many generated patches either cover only part of the developer-intended scope or partially overlap with it; in some cases, they further generalize beyond the reference patch. We further introduce historical-knowledge augmentation techniques that leverage prior LLVM optimization pull requests through retrieval and distillation, showing that they improve developer-aligned generalization and yield practical benefits when applied to real-world IR.

AI.tldr: The paper studies agents' ability to patch compiler missed optimizations, showing that agents often optimize specific cases but struggle to generalize fully. Historical knowledge improves their generalization effectiveness.
AI.motivation: Optimizing missed optimizations in compilers is labor-intensive, and understanding how well automated agents perform this work could reduce developers' efforts.
AI.method: Constructed a benchmark of LLVM missed optimization cases; analyzed agent-generated patches, compared them to developer patches; introduced techniques for leveraging historical knowledge from past LLVM optimization pull requests.
AI.result: Agents often patch given cases well but fail to generalize effectively to similar cases. Historical-knowledge augmentation improves alignment with developer-intended generalization.
AI.conclusion: Coding agents show promise in compiler optimization tasks, but their capabilities can be improved by leveraging historical knowledge for broader generalization and practical application.

title: Mastermind: Strategy-grounded Learning for Repository-Scale Vulnerability Reproduction
summary: Repository-level vulnerability reproduction is a demanding software engineering (SE) task: an agent must inspect a codebase, infer the input grammar that reaches a vulnerable path, construct a proof-of-conceptv(PoC), and verify that the crash disappears on the patched build. Recent LLM agents can often execute these steps when the approach is correct, yet they still fail by choosing the wrong strategy. This paper argues that strategy, rather than the full action trajectory, is the right learning unit for such SE agents: it is compact enough to optimize, concrete enough to guide execution, and stable enough to store and reuse across attempts. We present Mastermind, a dual-loop framework that separates transferable strategy learning from task-specific experience. A trainable planner learns reusable vulnerability-reproduction strategies through SFT and milestone-based GRPO, while an experience loop maintains task-local strategy records that guide subsequent attempts. The planner is trained independently of the executor, allowing strategy learning to improve multiple frozen executors without modifying their action-generation capability. We evaluate Mastermind on CyberGym using 260 training tasks and 200 held-out evaluation tasks. With GPT-5.5 as the frozen executor, Mastermind achieves an 84.5% pass rate, outperforming open-book PoC context (60.0%), Best-of-8 sampling (63.0%), and iterative improvement (77.0%). The same planner also improves GPT-5.4 mini and GLM~5.1 from 45.0% and 58.5% to 60.0% and 71.0%. These results demonstrate that learning high-level strategies is an effective and transferable mechanism for improving repository-scale SE agents.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Mastermind, a framework focused on improving software engineering agents by emphasizing high-level strategy learning for vulnerability reproduction tasks, resulting in higher success rates compared to existing methods.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of repository-level vulnerability reproduction where agents fail due to poor strategy rather than execution issues, highlighting the importance of reusable and transferable strategies.
AI.method: The Mastermind framework employs a dual-loop system: a trainable planner learns high-level strategies using supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and milestone-based GRPO, while a separate experience loop refines task-specific execution. The strategy is decoupled from execution, enabling cross-task learning and improvement of multiple executors.
AI.result: Mastermind achieves a pass rate of 84.5%, outperforming other approaches like open-book PoC context (60.0%), Best-of-8 sampling (63.0%), and iterative improvement (77.0%). It also enhances performance across different executors, such as GPT-5.4 mini and GLM~5.1.
AI.conclusion: High-level, transferable strategy learning significantly boosts SE agent performance in vulnerability reproduction tasks, validating Mastermind’s effectiveness and generalizability.

title: LASER: A Corrective Lens for LVLMs via Visual Attention Preservation and Sink Suppression
comment: The 19th European Conference on Computer Vision (ECCV 2026)
summary: Large vision-language models (LVLMs) exhibit strong reasoning ability but suffer from visual forgetting during long-horizon decoding, where attention progressively drifts away from visual evidence. Existing methods largely treat this issue as a late-stage attention decay problem or attempt to mitigate it through heuristic reminders or post-hoc attention lifting. Through systematic empirical analysis, we find that performance degradation under visual forgetting is largely driven by two overlooked factors: early-stage attention decay disrupts evidence acquisition, and attention concentration on a subset of task-irrelevant visual sink tokens. Motivated by these insights, we propose LASER, a post-training framework that regulates both the visual attention trajectory and intra-visual token attention distribution during reasoning. Technically, LASER introduces two complementary rewards: a Visual Grounding Reward, which encourages the model to maintain attention on semantically salient visual tokens throughout decoding, and a Sink Suppression Reward, which penalizes excessive attention concentration on visual sink tokens. Together, these rewards preserve early-stage grounding while preventing attention collapse onto uninformative regions. Extensive experiments on eight benchmark datasets demonstrate that LASER consistently outperforms strong baselines, validating attention-aware training as an effective remedy for visual forgetting.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces LASER, a framework to combat visual forgetting in large vision-language models (LVLMs) by regulating attention distribution and grounding.
AI.motivation: The study aims to address the issue of visual forgetting in LVLMs during long-horizon decoding, where attention shifts from informative visual evidence to irrelevant elements, which prior methods inadequately solve.
AI.method: LASER employs two rewards: a Visual Grounding Reward to maintain attention on essential visual tokens and a Sink Suppression Reward to discourage attention on irrelevant visual tokens. This approach adjusts attention during reasoning to enhance efficacy.
AI.result: Experiments using eight benchmark datasets demonstrate that LASER outperforms existing methods in mitigating visual forgetting, proving its effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: The use of attention-aware training, as implemented in LASER, significantly improves LVLM performance by addressing both early-stage attention decay and undue focus on irrelevant tokens.

title: QuadRocket: An Aerial Robotic Testbed for Adaptive Thrust-Vector Control of Rocket-Like Vehicles
comment: Paper accepted for publication in IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
summary: This paper presents QuadRocket, a quadrotor-based rocket prototype that provides a low-cost, low-risk platform for validating advanced thrust-vector control strategies for launch vehicle-type systems. The prototype consists of a cylindrical main body mounted on top of a quadrotor through a universal joint, forming a flying inverted pendulum with non-negligible inertia. For control design, the coupled system is modeled as a single axisymmetric rigid body actuated by a vectored force applied along its longitudinal axis. A reduced-attitude representation on the two sphere is adopted to explicitly exploit the vehicle's axial symmetry and to decouple yaw from the thrust-vector direction. On this model, we derive an adaptive backstepping controller that achieves almost global trajectory tracking in the presence of unknown constant disturbances, while a control-point transformation mitigates non minimum-phase behavior. The quadrotor is then treated as a thrust vector actuator, and a dynamic-surface-based attitude controller is designed to track the desired thrust-vector, accounting for actuation dynamics and avoiding explicit differentiation of virtual control signals. The complete architecture is evaluated in simulation and validated experimentally in an indoor motion-capture arena. Results demonstrate accurate trajectory tracking, effective disturbance compensation, and confirm the suitability of the QuadRocket as a versatile testbed for thrust-vector-controlled robotic vehicles.

AI.tldr: QuadRocket is a low-cost and low-risk quadrotor-based rocket prototype designed to test and validate advanced thrust-vector control strategies for launch vehicle systems.
AI.motivation: To create an accessible and low-risk platform for testing advanced thrust-vector control strategies used in launch vehicles.
AI.method: The study presents a novel control method using a cylindrical main body on a quadrotor, while modeling the system as an inverted pendulum. The approach incorporates an adaptive backstepping controller and dynamic-surface-based techniques to ensure effective trajectory tracking under disturbances.
AI.result: The proposed system demonstrated accurate trajectory tracking, effective compensation for unknown disturbances, and validated the potential of QuadRocket as a testbed in both simulation and experimental setups.
AI.conclusion: The research successfully introduces QuadRocket as a robust experimental platform for testing thrust-vector control strategies, showcasing its versatility and practical performance.

title: Object Aligner: A Configurable JSON Schema Similarity Score for Graphs, Applied to LLM Prompt Optimization
comment: 28 pages, This is a submitted version of a manuscript under review at IEEE Access; it has not been peer reviewed
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are often asked to produce JSON conforming to a fixed schema, powering information extraction, tool calling, agentic planning, and knowledge-graph construction. Measuring how closely an output matches a gold reference is essential yet surprisingly hard: exact match is brittle, text similarity ignores structure, and an LLM judge is expensive, opaque, and non-deterministic. We address this with Object Aligner (OA), an open-source Python library that scores two JSON objects deterministically by recursively aligning their trees (the Hungarian algorithm for unordered collections, sequence alignment for ordered ones) and awarding partial credit at the granularity the schema declares. The Object Aligner is configured entirely through a set of JSON Schema extensions, so adapting it to a new task involves annotating a schema rather than writing code. Complex structured data, however, are rarely flat trees: records may form graphs or hypergraphs keyed by arbitrary identifiers, breaking the assumptions of prior similarity metrics. Our central contribution, referential alignment, closes this gap by inferring a bijection between gold and candidate identifiers and scoring every reference through it, so the score is invariant to relabeling. Since recovering this bijection exactly is graph isomorphism, the Object Aligner approximates it with Weisfeiler-Leman color refinement. An order-sensitive sequence regime targets ranking and planning. Since the same alignment localizes every mismatch, the Object Aligner emits ranked repair suggestions at no extra cost. Used as a reward inside the GEPA prompt optimizer, Object Aligner helps or stays neutral across all datasets.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces "Object Aligner" (OA), a Python library for deterministically scoring JSON outputs by aligning object trees, enabling partial credit scoring within schemas and solving reference-based key matching challenges.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address challenges in evaluating JSON outputs from language models, where existing methods are either brittle, simplistic, or opaque, especially for complex and structured data.
AI.method: The proposed method uses tree alignment (Hungarian algorithm, sequence alignment), JSON schema extensions for configuration, and referential alignment to handle graph-like structures by approximating a key mapping through Weisfeiler-Leman color refinement.
AI.result: Object Aligner handles JSON evaluation across diverse tasks, provides local mismatch diagnostics, and improves or maintains performance in a GEPA optimizer setting.
AI.conclusion: Object Aligner offers a structured, transparent, and flexible JSON evaluation tool, with advanced handling of complex structures like graphs, overcoming the traditional limits of text similarity and exact match approaches.

title: Reasoning effort, not tool access, buys first-try reliability in agentic code generation: an observational study
comment: 22 pages, 5 figures, 10 tables. Dataset and evaluation artifacts: https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.21134406
summary: Agentic coding assistants are increasingly given extra capabilities, such as browser based testing tools and design oriented system prompts, on the assumption that more capability yields better software. This study tested that assumption directly. Ninety independent agent runs built the same application, a real time retrospective board, from one detailed specification, each scored on a fixed 14 criterion functional rubric (42 point maximum) and a visual quality review. The runs spanned several model generations, two agent harnesses, two reasoning effort levels, a testing tool, and two design oriented prompts. Capability tier dominated: frontier models clustered near the ceiling while a low cost local model fell to 24 to 37 points. A criterion level analysis revealed what run totals conceal. Container deployment was the dominant defect, failing first try in 44 percent of runs, with its failure rate shifting sharply across model generations while mean totals moved less than a point. The testing tool raised cost by 42 to 68 percent without improving functional score or reliability, even on interface visible criteria. Raising reasoning effort from High to xHigh lifted first try perfect runs from 28 percent to 89 percent and cut corrective prompts about five fold, for 9 to 29 percent more cost. A design oriented prompt raised visual quality, 4.5 versus 3.0 on a 5 point scale, without lifting function, and a one paragraph paraphrase of its directive reproduced the entire lift. The practical lesson is to match the fix to the failure: most first run failures came from weak reasoning, which a stronger model or more effort prevents, not from visible flaws a checking tool would catch.

AI.tldr: The study evaluates agentic coding assistants' efficacy by testing various configurations on software-building tasks and finds that enhanced reasoning tiers and frontier models outperform additional tools and lower-tier models.
AI.motivation: The motivation was to assess whether adding capabilities to agentic coding assistants, such as design-oriented prompts and testing tools, directly improves the quality and reliability of generated software.
AI.method: The study involved 90 independent runs of coding assistants tasked with creating a real-time retrospective board application, using a detailed specification. These runs varied model generations, harnesses, reasoning levels, testing tools, and design prompts. Performance was assessed using a standard functional rubric and a visual quality review.
AI.result: Frontier models performed significantly better than lower-cost models. Higher reasoning effort greatly improved success rates and reduced corrective prompts, while the testing tool increased costs without improving scores. Design prompts improved visual quality but did not affect functionality.
AI.conclusion: To improve outcomes, apply fixes that address specific deficiencies. Weak reasoning skills in coding assistants were the major source of failure and were better resolved by stronger models or higher reasoning effort than by tools like testing features.

title: SimWorlds: A Multi-Agent System for Dynamic 3D Scene Creation
summary: LLM agents are increasingly used to translate natural language into 3D scenes in a procedural way, but existing systems focus on static output. Dynamic 4D scenes from text alone, in which liquids flow, particles emit, rigid bodies cascade, and articulated mechanisms move, remain largely unexplored despite their value as editable content and as physics-grounded training data for video generation and embodied AI. Two challenges set the dynamic case apart from static text-to-scene work: an agent must jointly coordinate spatial layout, multiple physics solvers, temporal sequencing, camera, and lighting in a single coherent scene, and verifying motion correctness from rendered video is fundamentally harder than judging a single image. We present SimWorlds: a multi-agent framework that produces dynamic, editable 4D scenes from text, with Blender-specific procedural knowledge, a planner-coder-reviewer workflow driving a fixed ordered sequence of construction stages, a layered scene protocol enforced by a deterministic verifier, and a runtime-state inspection tool suite that catches mechanism failures the rendered image cannot reveal. We also introduce 4DBuildBench, a benchmark for assessing both visual fidelity and physical consistency of the procedural dynamic 3D scenes generated from text prompts. Experiments show that SimWorlds outperforms prior dynamic Blender generation baselines.

AI.tldr: SimWorlds is a multi-agent framework designed to translate text prompts into editable, dynamic 4D scenes featuring flowing liquids, moving objects, and more in Blender. It outperforms prior dynamic scene generation systems.
AI.motivation: While existing text-to-scene systems focus primarily on generating static 3D scenarios, there is a need to explore dynamic 4D scenes, where temporal and physical correctness is vital for applications like video generation and embodied AI.
AI.method: SimWorlds employs a multi-agent system with a structured planner-coder-reviewer workflow to generate 4D scenes in Blender. It uses a deterministic verifier to ensure physical accuracy and visual fidelity and a benchmark named 4DBuildBench to evaluate generated scenes.
AI.result: SimWorlds demonstrates better performance than previous methods for generating dynamic 3D scenes in Blender, achieving higher visual quality and physical realism.
AI.conclusion: The SimWorlds framework successfully addresses the challenges of creating complex, dynamic 4D scenes from text prompts, paving the way for better tools and datasets for video and AI applications.

title: Consistent Scene Understanding in 3D Gaussian Splatting via Multi-Cue Mask Refinement
summary: Reliable instance-level scene understanding is a fundamental prerequisite for object-level interactions and high-fidelity 3D representations. While current methods often leverage 2D foundation segmentation models to obtain these priors, their 2D-centric design typically yields fragmented masks and inconsistent predictions across different views. To address these issues, we propose a novel framework that produces consistent 2D instance masks to guide the optimization of 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) feature fields. Our framework consists of three main stages. (1) Multi-Cue Extraction that generates synergistic semantic, geometric, and structural priors from input images. (2) Multi-Cue-Guided Mask Merging process that consolidates fragmented masks using a composite merge score derived from semantic, depth, and edge cues. (3) Cross-View Mask Matching that establishes globally consistent identity assignments across all viewpoints. By transforming viewpoint-specific segments into coherent 3D primitives, our approach enables stable 3D instance segmentation and effective downstream editing tasks. Experiments demonstrate that our method significantly improves cross-view consistency and segmentation stability over existing baselines while maintaining high-fidelity photometric reconstruction.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a framework to improve 3D instance segmentation by addressing inconsistencies in existing 2D-centric methods.
AI.motivation: Current methods for 3D instance segmentation rely on 2D segmentation models, which often produce fragmented and inconsistent 2D masks across views, limiting their utility for 3D scene understanding.
AI.method: The proposed framework involves: (1) Multi-Cue Extraction for semantic, geometric, and structural priors; (2) Multi-Cue-Guided Mask Merging for combining fragmented masks using cues like semantic, depth, and edge data; (3) Cross-View Mask Matching to ensure global consistency across view-specific segments.
AI.result: The framework achieves improved cross-view consistency and segmentation stability compared to existing baselines, while also maintaining high-quality photometric reconstructions.
AI.conclusion: By transforming 2D segments into coherent 3D primitives, the proposed method enables reliable 3D instance segmentation and supports advanced 3D editing tasks.

title: CALM: Interpretable Cross-Modal Alignment for Biomarker Discovery from Unpaired Data
summary: The interaction between brain structure and genetic influences is key to understanding neuropsychiatric disorders. However, most large-scale datasets are unimodal, providing either neuroimaging or genetics data. We propose CALM, a framework that learns interpretable associations between brain ROIs and genetic pathways from completely disjoint populations. CALM aligns the two modalities in a shared latent space via linear projections that simultaneously match the class-conditional latent distributions and ensure group separability. These projections provide interpretable pathway--ROI associations. When trained on unimodal imaging and genetics datasets, CALM generalizes to an unseen paired dataset, outperforming several state-of-the-art methods and ablation baselines. We also demonstrate stability of the learned associations against a paired baseline. Our experiments on autism spectrum disorder reveal immune and metabolic pathways linked to specific cortical regions and are consistent with established literature. Thus, CALM opens the door to leveraging large unimodal repositories for studying cross-modal interactions in brain disorders across disparate datasets.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes CALM, a framework that identifies connections between brain regions and genetic pathways using separate datasets for neuroimaging and genetics.
AI.motivation: Understanding the interaction between brain structure and genetics is essential for advancing knowledge about neuropsychiatric disorders.
AI.method: The CALM framework aligns brain region and genetic data into a shared latent space using linear projections that maintain interpretability and group separability.
AI.result: CALM outperforms state-of-the-art methods in generalizing to unseen paired datasets and provides consistent pathway-ROI insights, validated through autism spectrum disorder analysis.
AI.conclusion: CALM successfully facilitates the study of brain-gene interactions using unimodal datasets, offering potential for research into brain disorders leveraging disjoint data sources.

title: Controllable Sim Agents with Behavior Latents
summary: Realistic traffic simulation requires agents that imitate logged behavior and can also be steered along interpretable axes. Such controllability enables engineers to isolate variables, reproduce specific edge cases, and test autonomous systems without real-world risk. We introduce Controllable Neural Variational Agents (CNeVA), a controllable simulated-agent framework that learns to infer a per-agent Gaussian behavior latent from per-channel discounted returns via a closed-form conjugate variational update, conditioning a rectified-flow trajectory generator trained on a mixed channel-mask curriculum for classifier-free guidance. To tackle scarcity in reward signals, we propose soft eligibility gates that replace hard binary thresholds with smooth exponential decay, preserving the gradient signal for near-threshold agents. On the Waymo Open Motion Dataset, CNeVA attains competitive realism on the benchmark while exposing per-channel controllability that the higher-ranked imitation models lack. Speed- and acceleration-based steering produces monotone responses without stall-induced reward hacking. Safety controllability is monotone and substantial with the introduction of soft eligibility. We manage to achieve steerable map compliance under a context-residual return measure. Furthermore, our experiment demonstrates that steering metrics must be read alongside physical-plausibility guardrails to avoid reward-hacking confounds.

AI.tldr: The paper presents CNeVA, a framework for creating controllable agents in traffic simulations, demonstrating competitive realism and interpretability.
AI.motivation: To create traffic simulation agents that mimic realistic logged behavior while maintaining controllability for safe and efficient autonomous system testing.
AI.method: Developed CNeVA, which uses Gaussian behavior latents via a variational update, a rectified-flow trajectory generator, and soft eligibility gates for smooth steering control.
AI.result: CNeVA achieves competitive results in realism and per-channel controllability on the Waymo Open Motion Dataset, avoiding reward-hacking issues.
AI.conclusion: CNeVA advances traffic simulation through interpretable and controllable agent behavior, proving useful for testing autonomous systems in a safe, realistic environment.

title: Using embeddings to predict spoken word duration and pitch in Mandarin monosyllabic words
summary: Time-normalized f0 contours of Mandarin words in conversational speech have been shown to be predictable in part from their contextualized embeddings (CEs). The present study investigates whether CEs also predict spoken word duration for 7470 tokens of Mandarin monosyllabic CV words extracted from a Mandarin corpus of spontaneous speech. We show that CEs indeed are predictive for duration, above chance level, not only at the type level, but also at the level of individual tokens, as indicated by the results obtained with the type-wise and token-wise permutation baselines. We also show that the predicted durations are sufficiently precise to back-transform predicted f0 contours in [0,1] normalized time to contours on the ms time scale. The resulting predicted contours approximate empirical contours and also outperform a permutation baseline.

AI.tldr: This study demonstrates that contextualized embeddings (CEs) can predict the spoken word duration and time-normalized pitch (f0) contours of Mandarin words in conversational speech.
AI.motivation: To explore the predictive power of contextualized embeddings (CEs) for spoken word features, specifically duration and pitch contours, in Mandarin conversational speech.
AI.method: The researchers analyzed 7470 monosyllabic CV tokens from a Mandarin spontaneous speech corpus. They used permutation baselines at both type and token levels to quantify the predictive accuracy of CEs for word duration.
AI.result: CEs were found to predict word duration at above chance level for both linguistic types and individual tokens. Predicted durations enabled precise transformation of normalized pitch contours to the millisecond (ms) time scale, aligning closely with empirical data.
AI.conclusion: Contextualized embeddings serve as reliable predictors of spoken word features, enhancing understanding of tonal and temporal characteristics in Mandarin conversational speech.

title: HTTP REST API Structure Learning
summary: Application Programming Interfaces (APIs) are essential in software development, enabling web services, mobile apps, and microservices. However, their widespread use introduces significant security risks, highlighting the importance of API security. This paper presents HTTP REST API Learning (HRAL), a novel unsupervised anomaly detection approach that models the structure and behavior of API endpoints directly from network traffic, without relying on predefined rules or documentation. HRAL enables robust detection of malicious activity by understanding how APIs behave and flagging deviations as potential threats. We evaluate HRAL across varying levels of OpenAPI documentation detail and compare it with existing techniques. HRAL achieves strong performance, with an average recall of 82.07% and an F1-score of 87.24%, significantly outperforming alternatives when API documentation is limited. Moreover, our results approach the effectiveness of full API document definitions. When combined with signature-based rules such as the OWASP ModSecurity CRS, our system achieves 100% detection. These results highlight HRAL's effectiveness in real-world, partially documented API environments and its potential as a foundational layer for modern API security solutions.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces HRAL, an unsupervised anomaly detection approach for API security, achieving remarkable performance in detecting malicious activity, even in partially documented APIs.
AI.motivation: To address security risks associated with widespread API use by developing a system capable of detecting malicious activities without dependency on predefined rules or complete API documentation.
AI.method: The authors propose HRAL, which learns the structure and behavior of API endpoints from network traffic using unsupervised modeling, flagging deviations as potential threats. They also evaluate its performance under varying documentation scenarios and in combination with signature-based rules.
AI.result: HRAL achieves an average recall of 82.07% and an F1-score of 87.24%, outperforming existing techniques under limited documentation, and when combined with signature-based rules, it reaches 100% detection.
AI.conclusion: HRAL proves to be an effective method for detecting API threats in real-world, partially documented environments, making it a strong candidate for improving modern API security solutions.

title: Repair the Amplifier, Not the Symptom: Stable World-Model Correction for Agent Rollouts
summary: As agent planning moves from short tool chains toward persistent workflows with thousands or tens of thousands of steps, failures will occur inside large planning graphs rather than in isolated predictions. Replanning the entire graph after every mistake is neither computationally realistic nor desirable: full-graph replay consumes large context budgets, exposes the LLM to many irrelevant symptoms, and can degrade long-context retrieval. This paper studies the missing component in such systems: a world-model corrector that repairs the failed planning graph in place. We compare two families of correctors. The first is the common engineering approach: scan nodes and edges, choose a suspicious local region, and ask an LLM to repair it. We implement strong engineering LLM correctors and find that they can help, especially when given very large contexts. The second family is our approach, WM-SAR (World-Model Subgraph Amplification Repair): instead of scanning for visible symptoms, it works backward from subgraph amplification, identifies the nodes and edges that keep re-amplifying error, and sends only that causal subgraph to the LLM. Across graph simulations and LLM repair experiments, WM-SAR substantially outperforms engineering correctors under realistic token budgets, achieves near-whole-graph stabilization with a compact region, and gives the LLM a cleaner repair target.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates strategies to repair failures in large planning graphs using a novel world-model corrector, WM-SAR, instead of reprocessing the whole graph.
AI.motivation: Failures in extended agent workflows with thousands of steps lead to inefficiencies in recomputing and degraded performance; thus, better in-place correction mechanisms are needed.
AI.method: The study compares traditional local-correction methods using engineering approaches with a new approach, WM-SAR, which identifies critical error-propagating subgraphs using backward analysis and repairs them using an LLM.
AI.result: WM-SAR outperforms traditional engineering correctors in repairing planning graph failures during graph simulations and LLM experiments while using fewer computational resources.
AI.conclusion: Efficient subgraph-focused corrections with WM-SAR lead to superior performance and resource utilization over traditional global or local repair methods in large planning workflows.

title: Beyond Pixel Diffs: Benchmarking Image Change Captioning for Web UI Visual Regression Testing
summary: Visual regression testing (VRT) is a standard quality assurance step in modern software release pipelines. On every change, it re-renders user interface (UI) screenshots, compares each one against an approved baseline image, and routes any detected difference to a human reviewer who decides whether it is an intended update or an unintended regression. A widely used approach, especially in open-source and continuous-integration pipelines, is pixel-level comparison, which is semantically blind and treats rendering noise and genuine defects identically, producing large volumes of false positives that force developers and testers to spend substantial time and effort manually reviewing flagged differences at every release cycle. Industry tools apply machine learning to VRT, but lack public evaluation. More critically, no dataset or benchmark exists to support natural language descriptions of UI changes, a capability that tells testers what changed in words instead of leaving them to interpret a binary flag or a highlighted region. To address the gap, we propose a new task, Web UI Image Change Captioning (WUICC), which sits at the intersection of VRT and image difference captioning (IDC), and release WUICC-bench, its first dataset and benchmark for the task. We evaluate eleven representative IDC methods, together with two zero-shot general-purpose LLMs. We find that: (1) these methods tend to struggle in the Web UI domain due to its layout diversity, dense text, and fine-grained changes, and (2) yet the trained methods already suppress non-meaningful visual noise far more selectively than the pixel-level comparison VRT relies on, providing a solid foundation for future domain-specific research.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the task of Web UI Image Change Captioning (WUICC) and its first dataset, WUICC-bench, aiming to improve Visual Regression Testing (VRT) by describing user interface changes in natural language instead of relying on pixel-level comparisons.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the inefficiency of conventional pixel-level Visual Regression Testing (VRT), which produces numerous false positives due to semantically blind comparisons, causing developers to spend excessive effort in manual reviews. The paper aims to provide a machine learning-based framework to describe UI changes in words, which is more informative and efficient than pixel-level analysis.
AI.method: The authors propose the WUICC task, introduce the WUICC-bench dataset as the first benchmark for this area, and evaluate eleven Image Difference Captioning (IDC) methods along with two zero-shot general-purpose large language models (LLMs).
AI.result: The analysis reveals that existing IDC techniques struggle with the unique challenges of the Web UI domain, such as layout diversity, dense text, and fine-grained changes. However, trained methods already outperform pixel-level comparison by selectively filtering visual noise, demonstrating potential for further research.
AI.conclusion: Developing domain-specific methods for Web UI Image Change Captioning could significantly enhance the capabilities of Visual Regression Testing by reducing noise-related false positives and providing meaningful, textual descriptions of UI changes.

title: Message Passing Based Two-Timescale Bayesian Learning for Joint Channel and Memory Hardware Impairments Tracking
summary: Hardware impairments in massive multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) receivers introduce inter-symbol memory and inter-element coupling, severely degrading channel estimation. This paper employs a residual recurrent gated unit (RGRU) to model the intra-slot memory of the hardware impairments and proposes a message-passing-based two-timescale Bayesian deep learning (MP-TTBDL) framework for joint channel and impairment tracking. Owing to small-scale fading, the wireless channel varies rapidly across slots, whereas hardware impairments drift slowly due to hardware aging and environmental variations. To capture these distinct physical timescales, a fastvarying Markov prior and a slow-varying Gaussian Markov prior are assigned to the sparse channel and the network parameters, respectively. Based on a multi-slot factor graph formulation, a message-passing algorithm is developed. Specifically, the inter-slot messages admit closed-form updates, while the intra-slot factor graph, due to its complex recurrent structure, is partitioned into a channel tracking module and an impairments calibration module. The channel tracking module performs sparse channel estimation via turbo orthogonal approximate message passing (Turbo-OAMP), and the impairments calibration module updates the impairment parameters via a specially designed deep approximate message passing (DAMP) procedure, with the two modules iteratively exchanging extrinsic information through expectation propagation (EP) until convergence. Simulation results show that the proposed framework robustly achieves lower channel estimation error than conventional compensators followed by channel estimation across different online impairment scenarios and signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) conditions.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the impact of hardware impairments on channel estimation in massive MIMO receivers by introducing a novel deep learning framework combining message passing and Bayesian techniques.
AI.motivation: To overcome the degradation in channel estimation caused by inter-symbol memory and inter-element coupling due to hardware impairments in massive MIMO receivers.
AI.method: The authors employ a residual recurrent gated unit (RGRU) and propose a message-passing-based two-timescale Bayesian deep learning (MP-TTBDL) framework. By assigning distinct Markov priors for fast and slow timescales, a message-passing algorithm is designed incorporating Turbo-OAMP and DAMP procedures for iterative joint channel and impairment tracking.
AI.result: The proposed approach demonstrates robust performance, achieving significantly lower channel estimation error compared to traditional methods under diverse online impairment scenarios and SNR conditions in simulations.
AI.conclusion: The MP-TTBDL framework effectively addresses hardware impairment challenges in massive MIMO systems by leveraging advanced deep learning and message-passing techniques, offering improved channel estimation accuracy.

title: Embodied.cpp: A Portable Inference Runtime of Embodied AI Models on Heterogeneous Robots
summary: Embodied AI models now span vision-language-action (VLA) models and world-action models (WAMs), but practical deployment remains fragmented across model-specific Python stacks, backend assumptions, and robot-side glue code, especially on heterogeneous edge devices. Existing inference runtimes are designed mainly for request-response serving and therefore do not satisfy the runtime contract of embodied deployment: multi-rate execution inside closed-loop control, latency-first batch-1 inference on heterogeneous hardware, and extensible embodied interfaces beyond fixed token I/O. We present Embodied.cpp, a portable C++ inference runtime for embodied models. Based on an architectural analysis of representative VLA models and WAMs, Embodied.cpp captures a shared execution path and organizes it into five layers: input adapters, sequence builders, backbone execution, head plugins, and deployment adapters. The runtime provides modular multi-rate execution, latency-first fused inference, and extensible operator and I/O support, enabling deployment across heterogeneous devices, robots, and simulators through one backend abstraction. We evaluate Embodied.cpp on two VLA models, HY-VLA and pi0.5, and on a preliminary WAM benchmark using a LingBot-VA Transformer block. The VLA deployments achieve successful closed-loop execution with 100.0% and 91.0% task success rates, respectively. The WAM benchmark reduces block memory from 312.2 MiB to 88.1 MiB.
