Overall, the proposed framework enhances interpretability without sacrificing counterfactual quality, enabling more actionable explanations for rehabilitation movement analysis.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a novel framework for generating interpretable counterfactual explanations (CEs) in multivariate time-series classifiers, focusing on group-based analysis rather than individual channel-level explanations.
AI.motivation: Counterfactual explanations are often challenging to interpret in domains requiring semantic feature group analysis, such as rehabilitation, because current methods focus on individual channels rather than meaningful feature groups.
AI.method: The framework comprises two components: (1) Shapley-Adaptive (SA) group ranking to preserve CE validity and (2) Learnable Gate (LG) methods that optimize per-group relevance gates with perturbation masks to enforce group sparsity.
AI.result: Experiments on a rehabilitation dataset show the proposed LG method significantly enhances group-level sparsity, improves counterfactual validity, temporal smoothness, and efficiency while aligning explanations with clinical reasoning.
AI.conclusion: This framework makes counterfactual explanations more interpretable and actionable, supporting clinicians in rehabilitation movement analysis by providing concise, muscle-level corrective guidance.

title: Hidden Forgetting in Continual Multimodal Learning: When Accuracy Survives but Grounding Fails
summary: Multimodal large language models must continually adapt to evolving tasks and domains, yet standard continual learning metrics mainly measure whether old answers remain correct, leaving the stability of multimodal grounding largely unexamined. We study this overlooked failure mode and ask whether a continually adapted MLLM can preserve not only what it answers, but also how it uses visual, textual, OCR, chart, and document evidence. We identify \emph{hidden evidence-use forgetting}, where answer accuracy is retained while the model silently shifts toward different or less grounded evidence channels, and propose \textsc{RCL}, a replay-free reliance-constrained continual learning framework. \textsc{RCL} freezes the previous checkpoint as a behavioral reference, estimates teacher and student evidence-reliance profiles through counterfactual channel interventions, and jointly optimizes task learning, prediction preservation, and reliance preservation without adding inference-time cost. Across CoIN, COAST, MCITlib, and an evidence-sensitive multimodal stream, \textsc{RCL} consistently improves final performance and reduces forgetting over replay-free, PEFT, routing, and memory-assisted baselines, while substantially lowering modality reliance drift, dominant evidence flips, and hidden forgetting rates. These results suggest that robust continual multimodal learning requires preserving the evidence path behind correct answers, not merely the answers themselves.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a method to address hidden forgetting in multimodal large language models by focusing on preservation of multimodal evidence-use in addition to maintaining answer accuracy.
AI.motivation: Traditional continual learning metrics focus on maintaining answer correctness but neglect how models utilize multimodal evidence, such as visual or textual information. Over time, models can experience hidden evidence-use forgetting, where they rely on different or less relevant evidence even when answers remain accurate, which can undermine trust and reliability.
AI.method: The proposed method, called Reliance-Constrained Learning (RCL), uses a replay-free framework. RCL compares a new model’s behavior to a frozen reference checkpoint by estimating how the model relies on specific evidence channels using counterfactual interventions. RCL then jointly optimizes task learning, prediction consistency, and evidence-reliance preservation without increasing inference-time cost.
AI.result: RCL consistently enhances performance and reduces forgetting across multiple datasets and multimodal learning streams. It mitigates issues such as reliance drift and hidden forgetting better than key baselines, including replay-free, parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT), routing, and memory-assisted approaches.
AI.conclusion: To achieve robust continual learning in multimodal large language models, it is essential to preserve not only the accuracy of answers but also the underlying evidence pathways leading to those answers. The proposed RCL framework effectively addresses these challenges, highlighting the need for holistic metrics and techniques in this domain.

title: Rethinking Post-Hoc Calibration in Semantic Segmentation
summary: Reliable confidence estimates are essential in semantic segmentation, especially in safety-critical settings where overconfident errors can mislead downstream decisions. Yet modern segmentation models often remain miscalibrated. Post-hoc calibration offers a practical way to correct confidence estimates without retraining the segmentation model, but its use in dense prediction raises structural issues that are often overlooked. We study two such issues. First, adding a constant to all logits leaves the softmax probabilities unchanged, but several standard calibrators can still depend on this arbitrary offset. As a result, two logit representations encoding the same predictive distribution may yield different calibrated probabilities. We define translation-invariant (TI) calibrators as those whose outputs are unchanged under such shifts, characterize which common calibrators satisfy this property, and construct TI counterparts of shift-sensitive calibrators to isolate the effect of removing representation dependence. Second, post-hoc calibration is typically fitted by minimizing a likelihood-based objective, whereas segmentation models are trained with task-specific metrics such as Dice. This mismatch can cause calibration to alter class orderings and degrade the deployed segmentation map. We study decision-preserving calibration under argmax- and order-preservation constraints. Since enforcing these constraints collapses affine softmax calibrators to temperature scaling, we introduce class-conditional affine calibrators that can be made argmax- or order-preserving while retaining greater expressivity, allowing us to quantify the calibration-segmentation trade-off induced by decision preservation. Across natural-image and medical segmentation benchmarks, and under corruption-based covariate shift, matched comparisons show that TI variants generally improve calibration metrics, while decision-preserving variants prevent segmentation degradation and retain strong calibration performance. These results provide practical design principles for well-defined post-hoc calibration pipelines in semantic segmentation.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses issues in post-hoc calibration for semantic segmentation models, focusing on translation invariance and decision preservation, offering improved calibration techniques that maintain segmentation performance.
AI.motivation: There is a need to improve reliability in confidence estimates for semantic segmentation models in safety-critical applications where overconfident errors could lead to incorrect decisions. Post-hoc calibration methods are practical but raise structural issues that require attention.
AI.method: The study investigates two key issues: translation invariance of calibrators and the effect of calibration on decision-making in segmentation. It introduces translation-invariant (TI) calibrators and class-conditional affine calibrators that preserve segmentation decisions while improving calibration reliability.
AI.result: Experiments on natural image and medical segmentation benchmarks show that TI calibrators improve calibration metrics, and decision-preserving calibrators maintain segmentation performance while providing strong calibration.
AI.conclusion: The proposed calibration approaches provide practical guidelines for designing post-hoc calibration pipelines, addressing key challenges and ensuring both accurate confidence estimates and preserved segmentation decisions in semantic segmentation tasks.

title: Decomposer: Learning to Decompile Symbolic Music to Programs
summary: Musical performance involves executing a set of high-level musical instructions, yet recovering those instructions from the performance is a challenging inverse problem. We present Decomposer, a post-training framework for symbolic music decompilation: the task of recovering executable, editable music programs from symbolic music. We instantiate the task as MIDI-to-Strudel decompilation, where the model takes symbolic MIDI as input and produces a program in Strudel, a music programming language, that reconstructs the input when executed. The task poses two challenges: Strudel is a low-resource language with little naturally paired MIDI-code data, and optimizing faithful reconstruction of MIDI alone can collapse to unreadable note-by-note transliteration. We address these challenges in two stages. First, we construct Strudel-Synth, a synthetic corpus of paired Strudel programs and rendered MIDI, and use it for supervised fine-tuning. Second, we refine the model with reinforcement learning on unpaired MIDI, optimizing rewards for both MIDI reconstruction faithfulness and code readability. Our evaluation across synthetic and real-world MIDI benchmarks shows that Decomposer achieves substantially higher MIDI reconstruction faithfulness than closed-source LLMs while producing more readable and diverse code than the heuristic converter.

AI.tldr: Decomposer introduces a framework for converting symbolic MIDI music into editable programs in the Strudel language, balancing faithful reconstruction and code readability.
AI.motivation: The motivation behind this paper is to address the challenge of recovering high-level music instructions from performances (symbolic music), enabling editable and executable music programs to be derived from MIDI data.
AI.method: Decomposer tackles the problem using a two-stage process: first, supervised fine-tuning on a synthetic corpus of MIDI-Strudel pairs (Strudel-Synth), and second, reinforcement learning to improve MIDI faithfulness and code readability using unpaired MIDI.
AI.result: Decomposer demonstrates higher accuracy in reconstructing MIDI and produces more readable and diverse musical code compared to closed-source language models and heuristic methods.
AI.conclusion: The study provides a viable solution for symbolic music decompilation by developing Decomposer, which leverages both synthetic datasets and reinforcement learning to achieve a balance between reconstruction accuracy and code readability.

title: PACE: A Proxy for Agentic Capability Evaluation
summary: Evaluating LLM agents on benchmarks like SWE-Bench and GAIA can be expensive, time-consuming, and requires complex infrastructure. A single evaluation can cost thousands of dollars and take days to complete. In contrast, non-agentic LLM benchmarks that test individual capabilities (e.g., reasoning, code generation) are fast and cheap to run. In this paper, we investigate whether performance on expensive agentic benchmarks can be accurately predicted by the performance on a small, carefully selected subset of atomic evaluation instances. We introduce PACE, a framework that constructs proxy benchmarks by selecting instances from existing non-agentic evaluations whose aggregate scores most reliably predict model performances on agentic benchmarks. Given a pool of candidate instances spanning atomic capabilities, PACE fits a regression that maps a model's scores on a compact subset of source instances to its score on the target agentic benchmark. The subset itself is curated by combining two complementary instance-selection strategies, target-relevance local selection and globally informative global selection. We apply PACE to the 4 target agentic benchmarks in this paper, which yields PACE-Bench, the concrete proxy benchmark that we evaluate in the paper. Experiments across 14 models, 4 agentic benchmarks, and 19 non-agentic benchmarks show that PACE-Bench predicts agentic scores with leave-one-out cross-validation (LOOCV) mean absolute error (MAE) under 4%, Spearman correlation above 0.80, and pairwise model-ranking accuracy around 85%, all at much less than 1% of the full agentic evaluation cost. We further analyze the selected proxy instances, revealing which skills each agentic benchmark uniquely demands. PACE enables practitioners to obtain reliable estimates of agentic performance during model development, selection, and routing, without the overhead of full agent evaluation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces PACE, a framework to create cost-effective proxy benchmarks for evaluating LLM agentic capabilities using carefully selected instances from non-agentic benchmarks. PACE significantly reduces evaluation costs while maintaining high prediction accuracy.
AI.motivation: The motivation stems from the challenges of evaluating LLM agents on agentic benchmarks like SWE-Bench and GAIA due to their high costs, time consumption, and infrastructure complexity. The authors aim to provide a reliable, scalable, and cost-effective alternative using non-agentic benchmarks.
AI.method: The authors propose PACE, which constructs proxy benchmarks by selecting non-agentic evaluation instances that can best predict performance on agentic benchmarks. This is achieved by combining target-relevance local selection and globally informative global selection, and fitting a regression that maps performance from a compact subset of instances to agentic benchmarks' scores.
AI.result: PACE was applied to four agentic benchmarks and tested on 14 models across 19 non-agentic benchmarks. It achieved under 4% mean absolute error (MAE), over 0.80 Spearman correlation, and around 85% pairwise model-ranking accuracy at less than 1% of the cost of full agentic evaluations.
AI.conclusion: The proposed PACE framework provides a reliable, efficient, and accurate method to estimate agentic benchmark performance based on non-agentic benchmarks. This advancement facilitates more practical model evaluation and development in the LLM field without significant resource burdens.

title: SFKD: Spatial--Frequency Joint-Aware Heterogeneous Knowledge Distillation via Multi-Level Wavelet Spectral Interaction
summary: Most existing knowledge distillation methods focus on homogeneous models (e.g., CNN-to-CNN), thereby overlooking the flexibility and potential of knowledge transfer across heterogeneous models. Due to intrinsic inductive bias discrepancies between heterogeneous models that cause spatial distribution inconsistencies, prior heterogeneous distillation methods often weaken or discard spatial information in heterogeneous representations. However, the spatial information in representations often encodes transferable global structural semantics as well as architecture-specific local details, and therefore should not be directly ignored. To better leverage the spatial information encoded in heterogeneous representations, we propose a Spatial-Frequency Joint-Aware Heterogeneous Knowledge Distillation framework (SFKD). By leveraging the complementary properties of wavelet transform spatial locality and Fourier representations in characterizing global energy distributions, we first apply multi-level discrete wavelet transform to explicitly decouple spatial information. The resulting wavelet sub-bands are further refined by a dual-stream dual-stage refinement module, and finally combined with a Gaussian-filtered frequency loss to selectively capture informative global information. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmark datasets under both homogeneous and heterogeneous models demonstrate the superiority of our method.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a novel framework, SFKD, for knowledge distillation across heterogeneous models by integrating spatial-frequency information using wavelet and Fourier transforms.
AI.motivation: Existing knowledge distillation methods mostly cater to homogeneous models and fail to maximize the potential of heterogeneous model knowledge transfer, particularly when handling spatial information in representations.
AI.method: The proposed SFKD framework uses wavelet transforms to decompose spatial information and combines this with a dual-stream refinement module and Gaussian-filtered frequency loss to extract both local and global features effectively.
AI.result: The proposed framework achieves superior performance on various benchmark datasets for both homogeneous and heterogeneous model scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The SFKD framework successfully addresses spatial distribution inconsistencies in heterogeneous knowledge distillation, proving its effectiveness and flexibility in leveraging spatial-frequency information.

title: Learning the Supports for Categorical Critic in Reinforcement Learning
summary: Value functions are an essential component in actor-critic based deep reinforcement learning (RL). Conventionally, these functions are trained as a regression task by minimising the mean squared error (MSE) relative to bootstrapped target values. Meanwhile, in distributional RL, a distribution of returns is modelled based on the distributional Bellman operator. This work investigates the Gaussian Histogram Loss (HL-Gauss), a recent approach that reframes value estimation as classification by encoding each scalar Bellman target as a Gaussian-smoothed categorical target. Despite its potential, applying histogram-based losses to RL presents inherent challenges, most notably the requirement to pre-define a fixed support interval, which is often complicated by the non-stationary and stochastic nature of target values typically found in RL tasks. In this work, we propose an approach that dynamically learns the lower and upper bounds of the support instead of assigning them beforehand. We derive an objective that jointly learns these bounds whilst learning the categorical representation of the scalar values, and we show that this objective forms an upper bound on the mean-squared Bellman error. Our theoretical analysis further shows that this bound is tighter than that of non-learned supports of HL-Gauss. Empirically, the proposed objective enables stable adaptation of the support interval and matches HL-Gauss-based actor-critic algorithms on most continuous-control tasks whilst improving on a subset, without requiring a pre-specified support interval.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes dynamically learning bounds for a Gaussian Histogram Loss in RL value functions to address challenges with fixed intervals.
AI.motivation: Value estimation in RL faces difficulties due to non-stationary and stochastic target values when using fixed support intervals.
AI.method: This study derives an objective to dynamically learn the lower and upper bounds while jointly encoding scalar Bellman targets as categorical through a Gaussian Histogram Loss.
AI.result: Empirical results show stable adaptations of the support interval and equivalent or superior performance on a subset of continuous-control tasks compared to HL-Gauss-based algorithms.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic bounds improve stability and flexibility in RL value functions, advancing over fixed support methods for Gaussian Histogram Loss approaches.

title: Algebraic Model Counting for Global Analysis of Optimal Decision Trees
comment: Proc. Joint European Conference on Machine Learning and Principles and Practice of Knowledge Discovery in Databases (ECML-PKDD 2026), LNCS, Naples, Italy, 7-11 September 2026
summary: Ensuring model reliability in Explainable AI requires a global assessment of the hypothesis space. We propose a formal framework for the exhaustive analysis of optimal and near-optimal decision trees, called Algebraic Decision Tree Counting (ADTC). Inspired by Algebraic Model Counting (AMC) in knowledge representation, ADTC reformulates diverse analytical tasks, such as optimization, counting, and sampling, into a unified sum-of-products computation over a semiring $R$. While the hypothesis space of decision trees is doubly exponential with respect to the maximum depth $Δ$, our dynamic programming algorithm achieves $O^*(n^{O(Δ)})$ time complexity in the number of features $n$, where $O^*$ suppresses polynomial factors. To handle complex constraints consisting of multiple tree metrics, we introduce model behavior tensors that aggregate semiring values via convolution products over a tensor semiring. This algebraic approach efficiently constructs a model profile that captures the global landscape and trade-offs between criteria such as accuracy, size, and fairness. We demonstrate the utility of our software, emtrees, on real-world datasets, illustrating how ADTC facilitates evidence-based model selection in sensitive domains.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Algebraic Decision Tree Counting (ADTC), a unified framework for analyzing optimal and near-optimal decision trees with applications in Explainable AI (XAI).
AI.motivation: The motivation is to ensure model reliability in Explainable AI by globally assessing the hypothesis space of decision trees, which is critical in sensitive domains.
AI.method: The method employs a dynamic programming algorithm based on ADTC to reformulate tasks like optimization, counting, and sampling into algebraic computations over a semiring. It uses model behavior tensors for handling constraints and aggregating metrics.
AI.result: ADTC achieves efficient complexity in analyzing decision trees, constructs a global model profile, and demonstrates evidence-based model selection on real-world datasets using the developed software, emtrees.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that ADTC offers a robust, algebraic approach to analyzing decision trees, enabling informed and reliable model choices in applications requiring fairness and accuracy.

title: Towards Real-World Ultrasound Understanding: Large Vision-Language Models from Multi-Image Examinations with Long-Form Reports
summary: Large vision-language models (LVLMs) have achieved strong performance across many medical imaging tasks, yet their application to ultrasound remains limited due to its inherent complexity and variability. In this work, we revisit what is truly needed to enable real-world ultrasound understanding. Instead of introducing complex architectures or elaborate training strategies, we show that data scale and clinically faithful data alignment are the key factors. We construct a large-scale dataset of 1.5M real-world ultrasound examinations, containing 17.7M images, multi-organ coverage, and paired uncurated clinical reports. Crucially, we organize the data at the examination level, aligning multiple images with their corresponding reports to reflect real clinical workflows. We then fine-tune a standard LVLM using low-rank adaptation (LoRA) on this dataset without task-specific modifications. Surprisingly, this simple recipe already leads to strong performance across diverse ultrasound understanding tasks, outperforming prior methods designed with more complex pipelines. Beyond these results, we present model and data scaling analyses that provide insights into the role of scale in ultrasound LVLMs.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that data scale and clinically faithful alignment play key roles in ultrasound image processing rather than complex architectures. By constructing a large-scale dataset and using simple fine-tuning strategies on LVLMs, they achieve strong performance across various tasks.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to enhance real-world ultrasound understanding by identifying the factors that truly matter, as prior methods struggle due to complexity and variability in ultrasound imaging.
AI.method: The authors constructed a dataset with 1.5M ultrasound exams and reports, aligned them at the examination level, and fine-tuned a standard LVLM using LoRA without task-specific modifications.
AI.result: This approach achieves strong, diverse task performance in ultrasound understanding, surpassing prior methods with complex pipelines.
AI.conclusion: Simpler methods leveraging large-scale and clinically aligned data can outperform intricate setups in ultrasound imaging, paving the way for scalable and efficient solutions.

title: ADVENT: LLM-Driven Automatic Predicate Invention for ILP
summary: Predicate invention (PI), the creation of new predicates to extend the hypothesis space, remains a critical bottleneck in Inductive Logic Programming (ILP). Existing methods rely on domain expertise and produce semantically opaque predicates, hindering adaptation to unfamiliar domains and cross-task reuse. We present ADVENT, an LLM-driven PI mechanism for ILP. ADVENT pairs LLM abductive generation with Prolog deductive verification, forming an iterative loop in which concrete execution results guide the LLM to refine candidate predicates. The mechanism leverages Large Language Models to identify implicit patterns in structured relational data and invent auxiliary predicates with meaningful names and definitions. Invented predicates and learned rules accumulate in a knowledge pool for cross-task reuse. Experiments on nine poker-hand concepts across seven LLMs show that LLM-driven PI achieves 58% success rate where ILP alone fails entirely, formal verification raises this to 80%, and the knowledge pool yields gains up to +31 percentage points, while producing human-interpretable rules. These results suggest that ADVENT offers a promising direction for automating predicate invention and enabling cross-task knowledge reuse in ILP.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ADVENT, a novel mechanism using Large Language Models (LLMs) for Predicate Invention (PI) in Inductive Logic Programming (ILP), achieving high success rates and reusable, interpretable rules.
AI.motivation: Predicate invention is a challenge in ILP as it often requires domain-specific expertise and creates semantically opaque predicates. This paper aims to simplify and enhance the process by leveraging LLMs to automatically identify patterns and create meaningful predicates.
AI.method: ADVENT incorporates an iterative loop pairing LLMs for abductive predicate generation with Prolog for deductive verification. It accumulates invented predicates and learned rules into a reusable knowledge pool.
AI.result: Experiments with poker-hand concepts across seven LLMs showed a 58% success rate for LLM-driven PI compared to no success for ILP alone, which rose to 80% with formal verification. The knowledge pool enhanced performance further by up to +31 percentage points.
AI.conclusion: ADVENT demonstrates the potential to automate predicate invention, achieving high success rates, cross-task knowledge reuse, and producing interpretable rules, indicating a significant step forward for ILP.

title: Evidence-State Rewards for Long-Context Reasoning
summary: Long-context reasoning requires models to locate, revise, and synthesize evidence distributed across lengthy inputs. Existing long-context RL methods usually reward final answers or static evidence extraction, offering little feedback on how intermediate actions change the model's evidence state. We propose Maven, a reinforcement learning framework with an editable evidence memory. Maven defines an answer-conditioned evidence-state value and rewards action-level state transitions: add actions are credited by marginal gain and hindsight contribution, link actions by evidence synergy, and drop actions by improved answer support after removing misleading evidence. These rewards are assigned to the corresponding action spans in GRPO. Across Llama and Qwen models on LongBench v2, LongReason, and RULER, Maven outperforms outcome-only RL and evidence-identification baselines, producing more sufficient evidence sets and lower distractor retention. Our results show that long-context RL benefits from optimizing stateful evidence navigation rather than one-shot evidence extraction.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Maven, a reinforcement learning framework that improves long-context reasoning by rewarding intermediate actions rather than just final outcomes, showing improved evidence synthesis across several benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current long-context reasoning methods focus on final answers or static evidence extraction, neglecting changes in evidence state during intermediate steps, which limits feedback and optimization.
AI.method: Maven introduces an editable evidence memory and assigns action-level rewards to intermediate steps: marginal gain and hindsight contribution for 'add' actions, evidence synergy for 'link' actions, and improved answer support for 'drop' actions. These rewards are applied in GRPO.
AI.result: Maven outperformed baseline methods in terms of producing sufficient evidence sets and reducing distractor information across benchmarks like LongBench v2, LongReason, and RULER, with significant improvements in evidence navigation.
AI.conclusion: Stateful evidence navigation, as implemented in Maven, offers critical advantages in long-context reasoning tasks compared to static, one-shot evidence extraction approaches.

title: Rank-Then-Act: Reward-Free Control from Frame-Order Progress
summary: We introduce Rank-Then-Act (RTA), a framework for learning control policies from expert video demonstrations without environment rewards. RTA trains a Vision-Language Model (VLM) offline as a progress-based ordinal scorer, using a Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) objective over shuffled frame sequences, which forces the model to recover temporal ordering from visual semantics rather than trivial time cues. Importantly, instead of using the scorer directly as a scalar reward model, we propose a correlation-based reward function for reinforcement learning: at each interaction window, we compute the Spearman rank correlation between predicted progress rankings and true temporal indices, yielding a bounded, scale-invariant learning signal. This design decouples reward learning from absolute calibration and enables stable transfer across tasks and environments. We evaluate RTA on discrete control benchmarks (PyBoy: Catrap, Kirby) and continuous control tasks (PointMaze, MetaWorld). RTA consistently matches or outperforms prior video-based reward learning methods and rank-based baselines, while demonstrating strong cross-task reuse of a single pretrained progress scorer. Our results suggest that correlation-structured supervision over video-derived ordinal signals is sufficient for policy learning, offering a scalable alternative to explicit reward design.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Rank-Then-Act (RTA), a novel framework for learning control policies from videos of expert demonstrations without environment-provided rewards.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to overcome limitations of traditional reinforcement learning that depend heavily on explicit reward design and calibration, especially in tasks derived from video demonstrations.
AI.method: RTA employs a Vision-Language Model trained offline as a progress-based ordinal scorer. Using a correlation-based reward function, it computes Spearman rank correlation as a learning signal during reinforcement learning, ensuring task transferability and stability.
AI.result: RTA showcases superior performance on discrete (PyBoy: Catrap, Kirby) and continuous control tasks (PointMaze, MetaWorld), surpassing existing methods in video-based reward learning and rank-based approaches.
AI.conclusion: Correlation-structured supervision over video-derived ordinal signals provides an efficient, scalable solution for policy learning, eliminating dependence on explicit reward design.

title: SUNTA: Hierarchical Video Prediction with Surprise-based Chunking
summary: Hierarchical state-space models (HSSMs) offer a promising approach to long-horizon prediction by segmenting sequences into temporal chunks. However, their performance hinges on how chunk boundaries are determined. While prior HSSMs typically rely on fixed-length chunking or similarity-based boundary detection, these methods often misalign with the intrinsic temporal structure of the data. We argue that chunking should instead be driven by prediction errors, which more directly indicate when longer-range context becomes necessary. Nevertheless, integrating surprise-based chunking into HSSMs introduces critical challenges, including hierarchical collapse during end-to-end training and the absence of surprise signals during open-loop prediction. To address these issues, we propose Surprise-based Nested Temporal Abstraction (SUNTA), a method that employs a decoupled training strategy to preserve surprise signals and uses internal inconsistency as a top-down surprise metric to determine chunk boundaries within imagined rollouts. Experiments on video prediction tasks in 2D and 3D environments demonstrate that SUNTA outperforms baselines, uniquely maintaining accurate predictions over 250 timesteps, whereas all baselines degrade within the first 10 timesteps.

AI.tldr: SUNTA introduces a novel approach to hierarchical state-space models (HSSMs) by using prediction error-driven chunk boundaries to achieve long-horizon accurate predictions.
AI.motivation: Traditional HSSMs struggle to align chunk boundaries with the actual temporal structure of data, as they rely on fixed-length or similarity-based chunking, leading to suboptimal performance.
AI.method: The proposed method, SUNTA, uses prediction error (surprise) to define chunk boundaries and addresses hierarchical collapse and missing surprise signals through decoupled training and top-down surprise metrics.
AI.result: SUNTA demonstrates superior performance in video prediction tasks, maintaining accurate predictions over 250 timesteps, unlike baselines that fail within 10 timesteps.
AI.conclusion: Prediction error-driven chunking significantly improves long-horizon prediction in HSSMs, and SUNTA offers a robust framework that overcomes key challenges in implementing this approach.

title: Sparse-Aware Vector Quantization for Bandwidth-Efficient Collaborative 3D Semantic Occupancy Prediction
summary: Collaborative perception extends single-agent perception by enabling multiple vehicles to exchange complementary perceptual information. However, it introduces an inherent trade-off between perception gain and communication overhead, which is particularly severe for 3D semantic occupancy prediction that relies on fine-grained spatial structures. Existing methods typically compress 3D features into 2D, causing severe spatial information loss, or transmit dense 3D representations, hindering real-world deployment. To overcome these limitations, we propose a bandwidth-efficient collaborative Vector Quantization Semantic Occupancy Prediction (VQSOP) framework. VQSOP employs a Sparse-Aware Vector Quantization (SAVQ) mechanism that exploits 3D scene sparsity to compactly encode informative regions, drastically reducing communication overhead while preserving complete geometric context. Furthermore, to enhance structural consistency and feature continuity, we design a Dual-Branch Adaptive Spatial Refinement (ASR) module that dynamically fuses local high-frequency details with broad contextual semantics. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance while reducing communication volume by up to 82x.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces VQSOP, a framework for efficient 3D semantic occupancy prediction in collaborative perception, achieving significant bandwidth reduction while maintaining high performance.
AI.motivation: Efficiently utilizing collaborative perception for multiple vehicles faces challenges due to the trade-off between perception quality and communication overhead, especially for 3D semantic tasks.
AI.method: The proposed VQSOP framework includes a Sparse-Aware Vector Quantization mechanism to encode sparse informative regions and a Dual-Branch Adaptive Spatial Refinement module to retain structural and semantic integrity.
AI.result: Experiments show state-of-the-art performance in 3D semantic occupancy prediction, with up to 82-fold reduction in communication overhead.
AI.conclusion: VQSOP successfully addresses the challenges of communication efficiency and perception accuracy in collaborative multi-agent systems, enabling real-world deployment of 3D semantic prediction models.

title: SABER: A Semantic-Aligned Brain Network Analysis Framework via Multi-scale Hypergraphs
comment: Accepted to IEEE International Conference on Multimedia and Expo (ICME) 2026;
summary: Effective brain disease diagnosis requires the synergy of brain connectivity patterns and high-level semantic knowledge. Existing methods, however, largely treat semantics from large language models (LLMs) as auxiliary features or supervision, limiting their direct role in decision-making and constraining classification stability and robustness. To overcome this, we propose a semantic-aligned brain network framework that actively integrates LLM-derived semantics into the prediction process. Specifically, ROI-level semantics are first incorporated via global self-attention to enrich node representations and provide whole-brain context. Multi-scale hypergraphs are then constructed to explicitly model functional subnetworks and multi-ROI interactions, addressing the locality limitations of traditional GNNs and capturing high-order dependencies. Finally, a decision-level semantic alignment mechanism selectively injects patient-specific textual embeddings into graph representations, enabling semantics to directly guide predictions without perturbing the underlying network structure. Experiments on public brain network datasets ABIDE and ADHD-200 demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, enhanced stability, and improved interpretability, particularly in small-sample settings.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an innovative framework merging semantics from large language models (LLMs) with brain network analysis for improved disease diagnosis.
AI.motivation: Existing methods poorly integrate semantic knowledge from large language models into brain disease diagnosis, hindering decision-making and stability.
AI.method: The proposed framework incorporates LLM-derived semantics through global self-attention, multi-scale hypergraphs for functional subnetworks, and decision-level semantic alignment for direct semantic influence on predictions.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance was achieved in brain disease diagnosis tasks on datasets ABIDE and ADHD-200, with increased stability and interpretability, especially in small-sample scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates the benefits of infusing semantic knowledge into brain network classification, advancing predictive accuracy and robustness.

title: ContextNest: Verifiable Context Governance for Autonomous AI Agent
summary:
Autonomous AI agents increasingly depend on external knowledge stores, yet most retrieval pipelines provide relevance without durable guarantees of provenance, version identity, integrity, traceability, or point-in-time reconstruction. We formalize this as context governance and present ContextNext, an open specification and reference implementation for governed AI-consumable knowledge vaults. ContextNext does not replace Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG); it supplies the governance layer beneath retrieval, determining which artifacts are approved, current, attributable, and integrity-verified before retrieval systems operate over them.
  The specification combines typed Markdown documents with metadata, deterministic set-algebraic selectors, contextnest:// URI references, SHA-256 hash-chained version histories, graph-level checkpoints, source nodes for live data through the Model Context Protocol (MCP), and audit traces of agent context consumption. These mechanisms let organizations reconstruct which knowledge versions informed an agent output and whether those versions were AI-eligible when consumed.
  We report first empirical results from two controlled experiments. In a stale-version attack isolating the governance-versus-retrieval failure mode, governed selection strictly Pareto-dominates BM25 sparse retrieval, with higher answer-quality pass rate (97% versus 93-90%) at about one-third the input-token cost. In a retrieval-determinism experiment over a 1,060-document corpus, deterministic selectors and BM25 return stable document sets across repeated identical queries (Jaccard 1.0), while a dense+HNSW baseline is non-deterministic on 80% of queries (mean Jaccard 0.611, worst case 0.210). These results suggest that context governance addresses failure modes retrieval quality alone is not designed to resolve. We release a core engine, CLI, and MCP server under open licenses.

AI.tldr: The paper presents ContextNext, a specification for governed AI knowledge vaults to ensure provenance, integrity, and traceability in AI systems, showing improvements over traditional retrieval models.
AI.motivation: Autonomous AI agents rely on external data, but current retrieval systems lack guarantees for provenance, integrity, and traceability. This motivated the authors to develop a governance system.
AI.method: The authors introduce ContextNext, combining typed Markdown, metadata, URI references, hash-chained version histories, deterministic selectors, and a Model Context Protocol to ensure knowledge traceability and integrity.
AI.result: The paper demonstrates ContextNext's superior performance over BM25 sparse retrieval in two experiments, showing improved answer quality, token efficiency, and retrieval determinism.
AI.conclusion: The study supports the claim that context governance addresses failure modes that existing retrieval models cannot handle, ensuring that AI systems consume reliable and traceable knowledge.

title: LiZAD: A Lightweight Zero-Shot Anomaly Detection Framework for Industrial Manufacturing
comment: Accepted at the IEEE International Conference on Omni-Layer Intelligent Systems (COINS) 2026
summary: In modern high-throughput industrial production lines, product configurations and visual characteristics frequently change, making it impractical to collect and annotate data for every new scenario. This dynamic setting makes Zero-Shot Anomaly Detection (ZSAD) particularly suitable, as it enables defect detection without requiring training on target-specific samples. Although recent ZSAD approaches show promising results, they are computationally intensive and thus unsuitable for deployment on resource-constrained devices. We propose LiZAD: a lightweight framework designed for real-time ZSAD specifically tailored for use on edge devices. The proposed approach pairs the dense and spatially aware visual features of DINOv3, crucial for precise pixel-level localization, with the highly computationally efficient text embeddings of MobileCLIP2. These features are then mapped into a shared latent space via low-memory trainable projection heads. Compared to six state-of-the-art ZSAD models, LiZAD achieves an average memory reduction of 61.5%, a parameter reduction of 74.6%, and a speedup of 3.02x in terms of latency. Despite substantial reductions in computational and memory costs, our approach maintains competitive anomaly detection performance, dropping the average P-AUROC by just 6.4% relative to the best state-of-the-art model across the VisA, BTAD, MPDD, and MVTec-AD datasets. Finally, it is successfully deployed on the NVIDIA Jetson NX and Jetson AGX edge devices and tested on the real production line of the Industrial Computer Engineering Laboratory (ICE Lab) at the University of Verona. The code is available at https://github.com/intelligolabs/LiZAD.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces LiZAD, a lightweight framework for real-time Zero-Shot Anomaly Detection (ZSAD) designed for edge devices, maintaining competitive performance while greatly reducing memory and computational requirements.
AI.motivation: The motivation lies in addressing the inefficiency of conventional ZSAD models, which are computationally intensive and unsuitable for resource-constrained devices, despite being ideal for dynamic industrial settings.
AI.method: LiZAD combines dense visual features from DINOv3 with efficient text embeddings from MobileCLIP2. These are projected into a shared latent space using low-memory projection heads, enabling real-time ZSAD capabilities on edge devices.
AI.result: LiZAD reduces memory usage by 61.5%, parameters by 74.6%, and latency by 3.02x while dropping the performance (P-AUROC) by only 6.4% on average compared to state-of-the-art models across four datasets. It is tested successfully on NVIDIA Jetson edge devices.
AI.conclusion: LiZAD demonstrates the feasibility of deploying real-time ZSAD on edge devices, combining computational efficiency with competitive detection performance, and addresses challenges posed by dynamic industrial production environments.

title: Population-Based Multi-Objective Training of Discriminators for Semi-Supervised GANs
comment: The 2nd International Conference on Federated Learning and Intelligent Computing Systems (FLICS2026)
summary: Semi-supervised generative adversarial networks (SSL-GANs) can exploit large unlabeled datasets while retaining a classifier in the discriminator, but their training is often unstable. This paper proposes a population-based evolutionary training strategy in which discriminator learning is formulated as a multi-objective optimization problem. Instead of aggregating the supervised and unsupervised components of the SSL objective into a single scalar loss, the method maintains a population of discriminators ranked by Pareto dominance, enabling the exploration of different trade-offs between classification accuracy and real/fake discrimination. This formulation aims to improve both roles of SSL-GANs: learning accurate classifiers and training generators capable of producing realistic samples. We analyze several variants, including an elitist strategy and a mono-objective ablation, to assess the role of multi-objective selection. Experiments on MNIST with limited labels show improved training robustness compared to SSL-GAN and CE-SSL-GAN state-of-the-art baselines, while the elitist variant consistently achieves the highest classification accuracy.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a population-based evolutionary training approach for semi-supervised GANs, improving stability and classification accuracy.
AI.motivation: Training SSL-GANs is unstable, and this study aims to balance classification and real/fake discrimination through multi-objective optimization.
AI.method: A multi-objective optimization framework with Pareto-dominance ranking for maintaining a discriminator population is employed. Variants like elitist strategies are tested.
AI.result: The proposed method outperforms baseline SSL-GANs in robustness and achieves higher classification accuracy, especially with the elitist variant.
AI.conclusion: The strategy improves training stability and enhances SSL-GAN performance, addressing trade-offs in semi-supervised learning.

title: AgenticDataBench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Data Agents
summary: Data science aims to derive actionable insights from heterogeneous raw data, unlocking the value of the massive amounts of data generated in modern society. Automating this process is essential to reducing labor-intensive efforts for data scientists and enabling scalable data-driven applications. Recently, large language model (LLM)-based data agents have emerged as a promising solution to automate data science workflows. However, the field lacks comprehensive benchmarks to rigorously evaluate these agents across diverse scenarios with fine-grained granularity. To address this gap, we propose AgenticDataBench, a comprehensive benchmark featuring realistic tasks spanning diverse domains with fine-grained ground-truth labels. This enables evaluations to capture the diversity and complexity of data science workflows and the detailed performance of agents. First, to cover diverse domains, we collect real datasets and tasks from 15 vertical domains, including 5 real-world B2B use cases from a leading fintech company. Second, to remove redundancy in real-world tasks and generate high-quality tasks for domains lacking real data, we introduce data science skills, recurring data-centric operational patterns, and quantify benchmark coverage by the number of skills included. Representative skills are extracted from large-scale task solutions on Stack Overflow using skill-aligned hierarchical clustering. Third, for real-world business tasks, we select task-solution pairs that maximize diversity in skill composition, ensuring broad coverage of practical scenarios. Fourth, to generate realistic tasks for devise domains without real tasks, we propose a systematic LLM-based task generation approach to create workflows and tasks based on these skills. Finally, we evaluate state-of-the-art data agents using our annotated benchmark and open-sourced testbed, providing detailed skill-level insights.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces AgenticDataBench, a benchmark for evaluating large language model-based agents on diverse, realistic data science workflows with fine-grained analysis.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of comprehensive benchmarks that can rigorously evaluate LLM-based data agents across varied, realistic data science scenarios.
AI.method: The authors curated diverse tasks and datasets from 15 domains (including real-world and fintech use cases), introduced skill-oriented task generation using clustering techniques, and proposed an LLM-based method to create synthetic tasks for underrepresented domains.
AI.result: A benchmark (AgenticDataBench) was created, featuring annotated datasets and tasks along with evaluations of state-of-the-art data agents, providing deep skill-level insights.
AI.conclusion: AgenticDataBench enables a detailed and robust evaluation of LLM-based data science agents, addressing the diversity and complexity of tasks while facilitating future advancements.

title: Enhancing Fitness Intelligence through Domain-Specific LLM Post-Training
comment: 8 pages, 6 tables, 2 figures. Accepted by the 12th International Conference on Big Data Computing and Communications (BigCom 2026)
summary: Scientific Fitness Coaching (SFC) is typically delivered by human professionals, making it costly and inaccessible to many. While recent advances in Large Language Models (LLMs) show considerable promise for more inclusive fitness coaching, directly deploying prevailing general-purpose LLMs in SFC reveals critical limitations. These models often lack sufficient domain-specific knowledge integration, leading to weak performance on complex SFC scenarios. In this paper, we introduce FitOne, a series of fitness LLMs (with 8B and 32B parameters) designed to improve reliability and domain specialization for SFC applications. Built upon the Qwen3 foundation models, FitOne is developed through a three-stage post-training pipeline consisting of continual pre-training, supervised fine-tuning, and reinforcement learning, using large-scale, high-quality datasets derived from rigorous knowledge engineering. We conduct comprehensive evaluations of FitOne on professional fitness certification exams, including ACSM-EP and NSCA-CSCS, as well as general capabilities such as knowledge reasoning and instruction following. Experimental results show that, while retaining strong general capabilities, FitOne-8B/32B achieves average improvements of up to 10.09%/9.29% and 12.73%/7.01% on the ACSM-EP and NSCA-CSCS exams, respectively, compared with the Qwen3 base models. Furthermore, in-depth ablation studies confirm the necessity of each training stage, highlighting the pipeline's effectiveness in balancing domain expertise enhancement with general ability retention. We believe this research advances LLM systems toward more reliable fitness intelligence and will inspire future research on developing domain-specific LLMs.

AI.tldr: FitOne introduces domain-specific fitness-focused Large Language Models to address limitations in general-purpose LLMs for Scientific Fitness Coaching (SFC), improving expertise and general capabilities simultaneously.
AI.motivation: Scientific fitness coaching is costly and limited in accessibility. General-purpose LLMs have limitations in handling complex SFC scenarios due to insufficient domain-specific knowledge integration.
AI.method: FitOne models (8B and 32B parameters) are based on Qwen3 and trained using a three-stage approach: continual pre-training, supervised fine-tuning, and reinforcement learning on large-scale fitness-focused datasets.
AI.result: FitOne showed average improvements of up to 10.09%/9.29% on ACSM-EP and 12.73%/7.01% on NSCA-CSCS exams compared to Qwen3, demonstrating enhanced domain expertise and general capabilities.
AI.conclusion: FitOne represents progress in creating domain-specialized LLMs for fitness applications, achieving a balance between general abilities and domain-specific knowledge, aimed at inspiring further research.

title: Personalized 4D Whole-Heart Mesh Reconstruction from Cine MRI via Multi-Scale Temporal Modeling and Differentiable Contour Rendering
summary: Accurate 4D whole-heart mesh reconstruction from sparse cine MRI is critical for creating cardiac digital twins, but remains challenging due to limited 2D slice coverage and the complex coupling between cardiac shape and motion. Existing methods often rely on intermediate contour fitting and typically reconstruct static, single-phase, or partial cardiac geometries, limiting their ability to capture full-chamber dynamics. We propose a novel end-to-end framework for reconstructing temporally resolved whole-heart meshes from multi-view 2D cine MRI sequences by learning an image-to-mesh mapping. The framework incorporates a differentiable contour renderer inspired by the Beer-Lambert attenuation principle, enabling anatomy-aware supervision of 3D+t mesh deformation through contour-based projection losses. To improve temporal consistency across the cardiac cycle, we further introduce a multi-scale temporal modeling module that integrates global cycle-level dynamics with local inter-frame coherence to generate smooth and physiologically plausible mesh trajectories. The proposed method achieved a whole-heart mean absolute error of 1.68 $\pm$ 0.31 mm and a motion jitter of 0.77 $\pm$ 0.17 $\mathrm{mm}/\mathrm{frame}^{3}$, outperforming existing methods with lower reconstruction error and substantially improved motion smoothness. It also improved 2D contour alignment across multiple cine MRI views and supported downstream proof-of-concept electrophysiological simulation. The code will be released publicly upon acceptance of the manuscript for publication.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an innovative framework for reconstructing 4D whole-heart meshes from sparse 2D cine MRI data, achieving high accuracy with reduced errors and improved smoothness.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to overcome the challenges in creating accurate cardiac digital twins due to sparse 2D MRI data and the complex interplay between cardiac shape and motion, which are not well-addressed by existing static or partial reconstruction methods.
AI.method: The approach uses an end-to-end image-to-mesh mapping framework incorporating a differentiable contour renderer based on the Beer-Lambert attenuation principle, with anatomy-aware contour-based supervision and a multi-scale temporal modeling module to ensure smooth and realistic mesh trajectories.
AI.result: The proposed method achieved a mean absolute error of 1.68 mm and a motion jitter of 0.77 mm/frame^3, outperforming existing techniques in both reconstruction accuracy and motion smoothness while also enhancing 2D contour alignment and enabling further simulation applications.
AI.conclusion: The framework significantly improves the quality of 4D whole-heart reconstructions from sparse cine MRI data, addressing key limitations of existing methods and supporting advanced applications like electrophysiological simulation.

title: Zeus: Towards Tuning-Free Foundation Model for Time Series Analysis
summary: We present Zeus, a unified tuning-free Time Series Foundation Model (TSFM) that delivers superior performance across diverse analysis tasks without any task-specific fine-tuning. Unlike prior studies that primarily focus on zero-shot forecasting but require task-specific tuning for other tasks, Zeus bridges this gap by addressing two fundamental challenges in multi-task generalization. First, to reconcile point-level granularity with long-sequence scalability, Zeus incorporates a multi-scale Transformer featuring point-wise tokenization and a U-shaped hierarchy, effectively balancing fine-grained fidelity with computational efficiency. Second, to accommodate varying inductive biases across different tasks, Zeus introduces Multi-Objective Temporal Masking (MOTM), a unified strategy that supports heterogeneous tasks (e.g., extrapolation, interpolation, and global abstraction) within a single framework. Extensive experiments across five representative tasks demonstrate that Zeus consistently achieves competitive results in tuning-free settings, underscoring its potential as a general-purpose TSFM.

AI.tldr: Zeus is a tuning-free Time Series Foundation Model (TSFM) that excels in diverse tasks like forecasting and interpolation without task-specific adjustments.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations of current TSFMs that require task-specific tuning and exhibit limited generalization capacity across various tasks.
AI.method: Zeus utilizes a multi-scale Transformer for balancing point-level detail with long-sequence processing. It introduces Multi-Objective Temporal Masking (MOTM) to handle heterogeneous tasks in a unified framework.
AI.result: Zeus shows strong performance in five representative tasks without the need for task-specific fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: Zeus is a promising general-purpose TSFM that delivers competitive results across a variety of tasks in a tuning-free setting.

title: Coding-agents can replicate scientific machine learning papers
summary: Scientific machine learning papers typically make computational claims, e.g., that the relative mean square error is less than 5% or that the 95% predictive credible interval covers the test data. A coding agent can be prompted to replicate those claims from paper materials alone, but the prompt does not by itself reliably preserve progress or check whether generated evidence supports the paper's claims. We introduce Paper-replication, a workflow that makes each selected paper claim a target with recorded evidence, and implement it as a coding-agent skill. The workflow makes the agent record those targets, reconstruct the paper's method, run computational experiments, link generated outputs to provenance and comparisons with the paper's claims, record where matched evidence appears in the replication report, and pass validation checks before completion. We evaluate Paper-replication on twelve independent runs across four scientific machine learning papers. All twelve workspaces pass the completion gate, and all 158 recorded targets are matched with report coverage. Even in this completed workspace state, repeated runs differ in how papers are divided into targets, in numerical fidelity to the source papers, in elapsed replication time, in the number of intermediate executions replaced before final evidence is accepted, and in the rules used to accept evidence. Paper-replication makes completion depend on workspace evidence and validation checks rather than on the agent's final message.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Paper-replication, a coding-agent workflow designed to replicate and validate computational claims in scientific machine learning papers through recorded evidence, method reconstruction, and validation checks.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to address the challenge of reliably replicating computational claims made in scientific machine learning papers, ensuring that the generated evidence supports these claims in a structured and validated manner.
AI.method: The Paper-replication workflow involves a coding agent that selects paper claims as targets, reconstructs methods, executes experiments, verifies results, links outputs to claims, and passes validation checks before declaring replication complete.
AI.result: The workflow was tested on 12 runs across 4 scientific machine learning papers, achieving 100% completion with evidence matching all 158 recorded targets, while discovering variability in execution across repeated runs.
AI.conclusion: Paper-replication provides a structured and rigorous framework for replicating computational claims, emphasizing evidence validation over mere agreement with the final output, thus promoting reliability in scientific research.

title: Conditional Co-Ablation: Recovering Self-Repair Backups in Transformer Circuits
summary: Mechanistic interpretability often relies on component-level interventions to discover how a model produces a behavior. This guides attribution, capability knockout, and model pruning downstream to operate by scoring each unit by the effect of ablation in isolation. Such first-order scoring is natural when component importance is additive, but becomes misleading when a transformer self-repairs: after a primary component is removed, a dormant backup can take over, muting the primary's measured effect while the backup itself appears irrelevant on the intact model. We recast this failure as a recovery task, conditional circuit completion, and introduce Conditional Co-Ablation (CoAx), a label-free, output-grounded score that asks how much each remaining unit's ablation effect grows once a primary set has been removed. This conditional growth exposes the second-order interaction that single-unit scores discard. On the GPT-2-small IOI circuit, CoAx raises backup-head recovery from 0.33 to 0.91 ROC-AUC, outperforming all baselines, including self-repair-aware gradient scores (best 0.82); counterfactual patching verifies that the recovered heads causally carry the repair. The same label-free procedure transfers to induction across eight models. Beyond discovery, the recovered backups correct self-repair-masked attribution, identify the components required for capability knockout, and yield repair-aware structured pruning scaling from 124M to 7B. Component importance is therefore not merely an isolated-unit property: in robust circuits, the components that matter can become visible only under the interventions that make them necessary.

AI.tldr: The paper presents Conditional Co-Ablation (CoAx), a novel method for identifying component importance in models, especially in cases of self-repair, improving accuracy in interpreting and modifying transformer circuits.
AI.motivation: Existing methods of analyzing model components rely on first-order scoring, which becomes inadequate when models self-repair, as dormant backup units can mask the true function of the primary components.
AI.method: The authors propose Conditional Co-Ablation (CoAx), a label-free scoring method that measures how much a component's ablation effect grows when a primary set of components is removed, exposing second-order interactions in the model.
AI.result: On the GPT-2-small IOI circuit, CoAx achieves significant improvements in identifying backup components used for self-repair, achieving an ROC-AUC of 0.91 compared to 0.33 from existing methods. It also outperforms baseline scores and generalizes to other tasks and models.
AI.conclusion: Component importance in transformer models is not isolated to single units but depends on their interactions within robust circuits. CoAx effectively reveals hidden components and improves understanding, attributions, and interventions in model interpretability and pruning.

title: A$^{2}$utoLPBench: An Auto-Generated, Agent-Friendly LP Benchmark via Inverse-KKT Construction
summary: Most LP-from-text benchmarks are static datasets of word problems written and labeled by hand. Once such a dataset is released, its size is fixed, its difficulty is fixed, and every problem can leak into the training data of future LLMs. We present \textbf{A$^{2}$utoLPBench}, a benchmark for testing LLM-driven agents on linear programming problems written in plain text. We first pick a feasible point and dual, then write down a problem for which that point is optimal and the objective value is known. The answer is known by construction, with no solver call and no human annotator. The evaluation environment bundles a reference solver-critic baseline and a Docker image whose usage instructions are written for an LLM-driven agent to read. With these in place, any agent can run the benchmark and get a calibrated score with one command. Because the benchmark is a generator rather than a fixed dataset, it has properties no fixed dataset can match: an unlimited supply of fresh problems, a difficulty knob set by $(n,m)$, ground-truth answers correct by construction, low LLM-side cost per problem relative to human authoring, repeatable scores across independent batches, and resistance to training-data leakage when fresh post-cutoff seed ranges are used.

AI.tldr: A$^{2}$utoLPBench is a dynamic benchmark to evaluate LLM-driven agents on linear programming (LP) problems generated in plain text, offering advantages over static datasets.
AI.motivation: Static datasets for linear programming from text are limited in size, have static difficulty, and are prone to training data leakage. The paper aims to address these limitations.
AI.method: The authors created A$^{2}$utoLPBench, a generator benchmark that constructs LP problems based on known optimal points and objectives without human annotation. It is coupled with an evaluation setup including a reference solver and Docker image for automated usage by agents.
AI.result: A$^{2}$utoLPBench provides an unlimited supply of LP problems, adjustable difficulty, ground-truth answers built by construction, lower costs, and resistance to data leakage compared to static datasets.
AI.conclusion: The approach offers a scalable and repeatable way to test LLM-driven agents on LP problems, overcoming shortcomings of traditional static benchmarks.

title: Assessing VLM Reliability for Medical Image Quality Evaluation Under Corruption and Bias
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are increasingly applied in medical tasks such as pathology description, report generation, and visual question answering. Medical Image Quality Assessment (MIQA) supports diagnostic accuracy and patient safety by determining whether images meet the standards required for clinical decision-making. Automating MIQA with VLMs may reduce workload, but their behavior under real-world conditions, where images may be degraded or textual context may affect judgments, should be further explored before deployment. We benchmark VLMs on medical image quality using the MediMeta-C dataset zero-shot across seven corruption types and five severity levels. We evaluate sensitivity to degradation patterns, the effect of corruptions on embedding geometry, and whether textual attributes (demographics, expertise, infrastructure, institution) alter scores. Across 16 VLMs and seven modalities, pixelation produced the largest score reductions (mean -20.58%, up to -34.4% for OCT), whereas brightness had limited effect (-0.81%). Embedding displacement was associated with score changes. Same-family models showed correlations of 0.67-0.83; some produced increases up to +31% for corrupted mammography. Textual attributes affected scores: institutional prestige raised them +17.15%, and equipment age lowered them -14.7%. The largest changes were +95.62% (InternVL-8B) and -37.7% (MedGemma). Current VLMs show limitations for medical image quality assessment. Pixelation, a privacy-preserving transformation, reduces performance, indicating a trade-off between patient privacy and reliability. Sensitivity to contextual metadata indicates limited objectivity and marks metadata as a privacy and bias source. Privacy protection and objective quality assessment are related requirements for use.

AI.tldr: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) were evaluated for their performance on Medical Image Quality Assessment (MIQA) using a benchmark dataset under various conditions, showing performance sensitivity to image degradation and textual metadata.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to explore the potential of automating MIQA using VLMs to reduce workload, while also addressing concerns about how degraded images and textual context may affect their reliability under real-world conditions.
AI.method: The study utilized the MediMeta-C dataset to benchmark 16 different VLMs across seven image modalities. The assessment involved seven corruption types and five levels of severity, focusing on degradation sensitivity, embedding geometry changes, and textual attribute effects like demographics and institution-related factors.
AI.result: The study found that pixelation can significantly reduce VLM performance (mean -20.58% and up to -34.4% for OCT), and embedding displacement correlates with score changes. Textual attributes like institutional prestige and equipment age also influence scores, showing biases and privacy concerns. Notably, some models exhibited extreme sensitivity, with score increases up to +95.62% and decreases up to -37.7%.
AI.conclusion: Current VLMs exhibit limitations in MIQA, with vulnerabilities to image degradation and biases caused by textual metadata. The findings highlight the trade-offs between maintaining patient privacy and ensuring reliable quality assessment, emphasizing the need for improved models before deployment in clinical settings.

title: Hybrid quantum-classical neural network for sentiment analysis
summary: Quantum machine learning has recently emerged as a promising paradigm that leverages the expressive power of quantum circuits to address complex learning tasks. In this work, we investigate the applicability of hybrid quantum-classical neural networks to sentiment analysis, a central problem in natural language processing. We focus on a dataset of tweets related to COVID-19, where the textual content is vectorized using TF-IDF and fed into both classical feedforward networks and hybrid architectures incorporating parameterized quantum circuits. Our results show that hybrid models can achieve accuracy comparable to the classical baseline, while exhibiting distinct learning dynamics, especially in terms of validation loss and accuracy, that suggest a richer representational capacity. Moreover, when applying transfer learning to an SMS spam classification task, the hybrid models consistently outperform the classical counterpart, achieving an accuracy increase of 15 percentage points (from 66% to 81%) on the spam class, demonstrating enhanced generalization. These findings highlight the feasibility of employing QML for natural language processing and point toward the potential advantages of hybrid models as quantum hardware continues to advance.

AI.tldr: The study explores hybrid quantum-classical neural networks for sentiment analysis, showing comparable accuracy to classical models and improved generalization in a transfer learning task.
AI.motivation: To investigate if hybrid quantum-classical neural networks can be effectively applied to sentiment analysis and to explore their advantages over classical models in natural language processing.
AI.method: The researchers used a dataset of COVID-19 related tweets, vectorized through TF-IDF, and tested both classical feedforward networks and hybrid neural networks with parameterized quantum circuits. They also applied transfer learning to an SMS spam classification task for comparison.
AI.result: Hybrid models achieved accuracy similar to classical ones on sentiment analysis but demonstrated a 15% improvement in transfer learning for SMS spam classification. They exhibited distinct dynamics in validation loss and accuracy, suggesting richer representational capacity.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid quantum-classical models are feasible for NLP tasks and show potential benefits, particularly in generalization for transfer learning, as quantum hardware evolves.

title: A rubric-based controlled comparison of frontier language models on expert-authored clinical reasoning tasks
summary: Multiple-choice medical benchmarks are increasingly saturated, and recent rubric-based evaluations such as HealthBench have shown that open-ended clinical performance is far from solved - its "Hard" subset top score remains 32%. We present a small, deliberately difficult evaluation dataset of five clinician-authored clinical scenarios spanning four specialties (anaesthesia, internal/family medicine, emergency medicine, and obstetrics), each accompanied by an atomic, weighted, MECE rubric (25-62 criteria per task; 184 criteria total) authored from a clinician-drafted golden answer. We evaluate three frontier models: GPT 5.4, Claude Opus 4.7, and Gemini 3.1 Pro. Mean rubric pass rates were 0.47 (Claude), 0.39 (GPT), and 0.37 (Gemini). The central finding is an inversion of clinical priority: the highest-weighted (weight-5, critical) criteria passed at only 32.4-41.7%, while low-stakes weight-1 criteria passed at 80-90%. 56 of 108 critical (weight-5) criteria (52%) were satisfied by no model. Three LLM autoraters reproduced expert met/not-met labels on 92.8-94.7% of 552 graded criteria. We position this as a methods-and-preliminary-findings contribution: the five tasks demonstrate a scalable, defensible pipeline ready to develop into a large-scale benchmark.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a challenging open-ended clinical evaluation dataset and evaluates three large language models, revealing significant shortcomings in addressing critical clinical criteria. It proposes a scalable benchmark development pipeline.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the gap between high performance in multiple-choice medical benchmarks and poor performance in open-ended clinical scenarios, highlighting a need for more difficult and clinically relevant evaluation benchmarks.
AI.method: The authors introduced a clinician-authored dataset composed of five complex clinical scenarios. They created a detailed rubric for evaluation (184 criteria in total) and tested three advanced models: GPT 5.4, Claude Opus 4.7, and Gemini 3.1 Pro.
AI.result: The models showed suboptimal performances, particularly on high-priority clinical criteria. Critical criteria (weight-5) scored as low as 32.4-41.7%, and 52% of these were not satisfied by any model. Autoraters closely aligned with expert evaluations over 92.8-94.7% of the time.
AI.conclusion: Despite some preliminary results, the study points to the potential for using its methodology and evaluation pipeline to establish a more comprehensive benchmark for advancing clinical AI capabilities.

title: MolSight: A Graph-Aware Vision-Language Model for Unified Chemical Image Understanding
summary: Using molecular large language models (LLMs) as a unified framework for understanding molecular structures and functions is emerging as a new trend in tasks such as molecular design and drug discovery. However, these models struggle to fully capture the visual representation of molecular structures, limiting their potential. While existing molecular vision-language models (VLMs) show promise, they still face challenges in structural alignment and lack the necessary topological modeling for accurate molecular understanding. To address this, we propose MolSight, a graph-aware vision-language model framework designed to enhance the understanding of molecular images by VLMs. MolSight integrates a Molecular Topology Module to inject chemical-bond adjacency information into vision tokens, and a Molecular Grounding Module to align visual features with chemical symbolic semantics. Our experiments demonstrate that MolSight significantly outperforms existing VLMs, molecular LLMs, and specialized tools across multiple chemical visual understanding tasks, achieving a new level of molecular image reasoning.

AI.tldr: MolSight enhances molecular image understanding by integrating graph-aware vision-language framework and aligning visual features with chemical semantics.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of molecular LLMs and VLMs in capturing visual and structural aspects of molecular data.
AI.method: MolSight introduces a Molecular Topology Module for adjacency information and a Molecular Grounding Module for semantic alignment.
AI.result: MolSight outperformed existing tools and models in chemical visual understanding tasks.
AI.conclusion: Graph-aware enhancements enable superior molecular reasoning in visual tasks, showcasing MolSight's potential in drug discovery and molecular design.

title: A More Accurate Algorithm Comparison through A/B Testing using Offline Evaluation Methods
summary: A/B testing is the gold standard for selecting the better algorithm in online services. While offline evaluation has attracted attention as a safer alternative due to the high experimental costs and the potential risk of degrading user experience and revenue in A/B testing, it is widely recognized that the estimation accuracy of offline evaluation is substantially lower. As a result, final selection decisions are typically made through A/B testing. Contrary to this conventional view, we reveal a counterintuitive phenomenon in which A/B testing can produce a higher algorithm selection error rate than offline evaluation. This occurs because the sample mean estimator used in A/B testing does not induce positive correlation, which is crucial for reducing critical selection errors, namely underestimating the truly superior algorithm and overestimating the truly inferior one. In contrast, offline evaluation methods unintentionally generate this beneficial correlation by relying on shared offline data when estimating and comparing the performance of multiple algorithms. Building on this insight, we propose an estimator that intentionally induces positive correlation to improve algorithm selection in A/B testing. The key idea is to introduce a hypothetical middle algorithm and to estimate the performance difference between algorithms A, M, and B in a stepwise manner using shared data at each step. This approach enables the application of offline evaluation techniques in each step, thereby inducing positive correlation and reducing critical selection errors. Furthermore, we derive the optimal middle algorithm regarding the resulting variance and analyze its advantages over existing methods through bias-variance analysis. Experiments on real-world data demonstrate that our estimator achieves the same selection error rate as existing approaches while using only one half of the A/B testing data.

AI.tldr: A/B testing, while standard, can lead to higher selection error rates than offline evaluation due to lack of induced positive correlation. The authors propose a novel estimator introducing a hypothetical middle algorithm to mitigate this issue, halving the required data for A/B testing.
AI.motivation: To address the high selection error rate in algorithm evaluation, which arises from the lack of positive correlation in conventional A/B testing compared to offline evaluation techniques.
