AI.method: The proposed SA-RDM-DC method employs a physics-conditioned drift field trained from zero-filled to fully sampled residuals. It incorporates image and k-space residual corrections, data consistency enforcement, frequency-aware supervision, and produces dense error maps with risk scores in the same pass.
AI.result: SA-RDM-DC outperforms benchmark methods like UNet-image-SENSE and MoDL in SSIM scores while achieving subsecond inference time. It preserves pathology region structural fidelity and reduces instability in meniscus predictions.
AI.conclusion: SA-RDM-DC is a robust accelerated MRI reconstruction framework that balances image fidelity, pathology preservation, runtime efficiency, and reliability by incorporating self-auditing capabilities.

title: Structured Gaussian Processes for Uncertainty-Aware Classification of High-Dimensional, Small-Sampled Omics Data
summary:
Classifying heterogeneous omics data remains a fundamental challenge in computational biology, particularly in high-dimensional, small-sample settings where nonlinear interactions dominate and class imbalance further complicates reliable prediction of minority phenotypes. While traditional kernel methods rely on feature abundance, they fail to leverage the known interaction landscapes of biological systems.
  In this work, we propose a structured Gaussian process classification framework that integrates graph-encoded biological pathways directly into the kernel construction. By propagating information along known interaction networks and combining this with abundance-derived features, the resulting classifier captures both quantitative measurements and topological context.
  We benchmark our proposed methodology on three publicly available gut and fecal microbiome datasets. To address severe class imbalance, we evaluate complementary strategies, including data-level resampling, threshold calibration, and confusion-matrix-based adjustments, and report minority-class performance alongside accuracy. The hybrid approach yields a performance gain over unstructured baselines and matches the performance of established benchmarks for similar datasets. Furthermore, the probabilistic nature of the framework naturally provides calibrated predictive uncertainty, enabling robust differentiation between confident predictions and ambiguous samples.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a structured Gaussian process classification framework integrating graph-encoded biological pathways and quantitative features, addressing challenges like class imbalance and nonlinear interactions in classifying omics data.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to improve classification performance for omics data by accounting for biological interaction networks and addressing high-dimensionality, small-samples, and class imbalance challenges.
AI.method: A classification framework is created by combining graph-encoded biological pathways into Gaussian process kernels, alongside complementary strategies like resampling and threshold calibration for class imbalance adjustments.
AI.result: The framework demonstrated improved performance over unstructured baselines and matched benchmarks, providing calibrated predictive uncertainty for confident and ambiguous predictions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively integrates biological topology into classification tasks, offering robust performance, improved minority-class prediction, and meaningful uncertainty estimates.

title: Tight Lower Bounds for the Multi-Secretary Problem via Bellman Certificates
summary: This paper studies additive regret in the multi-secretary problem, defined as the gap between the expected offline prophet reward and the reward of the best online policy. Prior work established \(O(\log T)\) regret for bounded-density distributions with connected support and \(O((\log T)^2)\) upper bounds for bounded-density distributions with support gaps. It was unknown whether the extra logarithmic factor is necessary even in the one-resource model. We prove that it is necessary. For a mixture of two separated uniform distributions at the critical capacity, the optimal regret grows at least on the order of \((\log T)^2\). Thus the existing \(O((\log T)^2)\) upper bounds for bounded-density gapped instances, including those implied by network revenue management models with continuous rewards, are tight in this simplest specialization. The same framework also yields a matching lower bound for gapped distributions whose gap-facing densities vanish near the support edges; this companion result is given in the appendix. The proofs use Bellman certificates: feasible solutions to a relaxation of the exact Bellman recursion. This framework converts lower bounds into explicit certificate constructions and identifies why support gaps permit larger regret.

AI.tldr: The paper proves that the additive regret in the multi-secretary problem for gapped distributions indeed grows as \((\log T)^2\), confirming the tightness of prior upper bounds.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to determine whether the existing \((\log T)^2\) regret upper bounds in the one-resource multi-secretary problem for gapped distributions are necessary, resolving an open question.
AI.method: The paper uses Bellman certificates, which are feasible solutions to a relaxed version of the exact Bellman recursion, to construct explicit proofs and analyze the gap between offline and online outcomes.
AI.result: It demonstrates a lower bound of \((\log T)^2\) regret for certain cases of bounded-density gapped distributions, confirming the tightness of the upper bounds.
AI.conclusion: The \((\log T)^2\) growth in regret is fundamental in the presence of support gaps, and the Bellman certificate approach explains why such gaps lead to higher regret.

title: Has This Checkpoint Been Abliterated? A Two-Signal Audit and Its Failure Map
summary: Can a platform tell, before deployment, whether an open-weight checkpoint has had its refusal mechanism stripped? Runtime guards cannot: they score generations, not the artifact. We combine two cheap internal signals, a reference-anchored activation refusal-gap and a weight-recovery energy of the base-to-candidate weight difference, into a threshold-free checkpoint audit. The two are negatively correlated and label-complementary: the gap supplies refusal-specificity and the weight energy supplies recall. On a 273-checkpoint registry spanning Qwen, DeepSeek-distilled Qwen, Llama, and Gemma, their z-sum separates 57 public abliterations from 37 benign fine-tunes, merges, and instruction-tunes at AUROC 0.95, significantly above either signal alone (0.84, 0.90), and a Youden-calibrated threshold transfers to held-out families at balanced accuracy 0.89 (FPR 0.11), missing only 4 of 57. We then map two failures, in order of severity: a spoofed reference evades both axes with no training (ΔW=0, \r{ho}=1 by construction), and a white-box owner trains a checkpoint past the threshold while it stays guard-unsafe and coherent. The audit is effective triage, not tamper-proofing: it presumes an attested reference, and its claims are bounded by the registry we evaluate it on.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a method to audit open-weight checkpoints for refusal mechanism removal before deployment, achieving high accuracy using two internal signals.
AI.motivation: The need to ensure that open-weight checkpoints retain their refusal mechanisms and to assess them effectively before deployment, rather than relying on runtime generation scoring.
AI.method: The method combines two internal signals: a reference-anchored activation refusal-gap and weight-recovery energy, which together form a threshold-free checkpoint audit. The signals are complementary and combined via z-sum.
AI.result: Testing on a registry of 273 checkpoints, the method achieved AUROC of 0.95, outperforming individual signals (AUROC 0.84 and 0.90) with balanced accuracy of 0.89 and missing only 4 of 57 cases.
AI.conclusion: The audit efficiently distinguishes between abliterated and benign fine-tuned checkpoints but relies on an attested reference and is not foolproof against intentional malicious alterations.

title: Fourier Preconditioning for Neural Feature Learning
comment: Accepted for publication in IEEE Signal Processing Letters
summary: Mutual information (MI)-inspired feature learning techniques are capable of generating low-dimensional embeddings that retain nonlinear dependence structures, but direct estimations of MI suffer from noisy probability distribution estimates in the low-data regime. The H-Score objective, computed from second-order statistics, provides a practical proxy metric for training feature extraction networks. We prove that H-Score is invariant to invertible transformations in the unrestricted functional setting, but becomes sensitive to input basis rotations under constrained approximation classes. Consequently, we study unitary preconditioning for H-Score networks and show that selecting an appropriate basis rotation reduces finite-width truncation error by concentrating predictive dependence into fewer dominant modes. We identify the fast Fourier transform (FFT) as an effective data-independent, low-cost preconditioner for approximately stationary processes, where spectral structure induces concentration of the cross-covariance singular value spectrum. We introduce training-free metrics based on spectral entropy and cumulative dependence energy to quantify basis suitability and predict downstream inference gains prior to network training. Experiments across eight multivariate datasets demonstrate that FFT preconditioning is particularly useful in resource-constrained regimes, achieving up to 50% normalized mean squared error (NMSE) reduction, while the proposed metrics correlate with observed performance gains and correctly identify cases where spectral preconditioning is detrimental.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how unitary preconditioning using the fast Fourier transform (FFT) enhances feature learning with H-Score networks, particularly in low-data settings. FFT reduces truncation errors and improves performance in multivariate datasets.
AI.motivation: Direct mutual information estimation struggles with noise in low-data scenarios, necessitating alternative approaches for nonlinear feature extraction.
AI.method: The authors utilize the H-Score criterion for training feature networks, incorporate FFT-based preconditioning, and introduce training-free metrics to evaluate data suitability for spectral transformations.
AI.result: FFT preconditioning significantly improved normalized mean squared error (NMSE) by up to 50% in multivariate datasets, and proposed metrics effectively predicted its performance impact.
AI.conclusion: FFT is a promising tool for feature preprocessing in low-data regimes, with spectral metrics offering valuable predictive insights into its applicability across datasets.

title: Low-Latency Task-Oriented Image Transmission with Opportunistic Spectrum Access
comment: This work has been accepted for presentation at IEEE SPAWC 2026
summary: Communication systems designed for reliable data reconstruction, rather than task-oriented communication, typically rely on separate source and channel coding and incur high latency under limited spectrum availability and fading channels. To address this, we propose a transmission framework with opportunistic spectrum access, in which the transmitter sends discrete latent representations learned via a vector-quantized variational autoencoder (VQ-VAE) over idle licensed channels using standard digital modulation. The AI-powered receiver is still able to reconstruct task-related information from the heavily compressed data. We develop a cross-layer latency model that accounts for compression, block errors, retransmissions, and stochastic channel access. Results on latency-accuracy trade-offs show that the proposed scheme achieves at least 79- and 3.3-fold latency reductions with only 5.7% and 2.4% drops in classification accuracy compared to benchmarks using conventional source and channel coding. The framework enables low-latency communication and reliable task execution even under limited spectrum availability and challenging channel conditions.

AI.tldr: A framework using vector-quantized variational autoencoders (VQ-VAEs) and opportunistic spectrum access offers low-latency communication for task-specific information reconstruction, achieving high efficiency under challenging conditions.
AI.motivation: Traditional communication systems prioritize reliable data reconstruction, focusing on separate source and channel coding. This approach suffers from high latency, especially under limited spectrum and fading channels, motivating the need for a more efficient, task-oriented communication method.
AI.method: The proposed system uses discrete latent representation encoding via VQ-VAEs, transmitted opportunistically over idle licensed channels using standard digital modulation. A cross-layer latency model accounts for multiple factors such as compression, channel errors, and retransmissions.
AI.result: The framework demonstrates significant latency reductions—79- and 3.3-fold decreases—while maintaining classification accuracy with only minor drops (5.7% and 2.4%) compared to systems using standard coding.
AI.conclusion: This novel framework effectively balances latency and accuracy, enabling reliable task execution over constrained spectrum and challenging channel conditions in AI-powered communication scenarios.

title: CausalSteward: An Agentic Divide-Conquer-Combine Copilot for Causal Discovery
summary: Learning causal models from high-dimensional data is a significant challenge, particularly in real-world settings where violations of core assumptions lead to causal identifiability issues. Although massive amounts of prior knowledge are available, and contain valuable causal information, effectively integrating this knowledge into the causal discovery process remains an open problem. We introduce CausalSTeward (CAST), a novel human-in-the-loop framework for interactively assembling large causal models. CausalSteward is a multi-agent collaborative system that tackles high-dimensional causality through a divide-and-conquer approach where large clusters of variables are iteratively partitioned and then separately analyzed. Our framework fuses prior knowledge with a data-driven approach by using tailored tools such as retrieval augmented generation and conditional independence tests. Finally, we use this work to examine the capabilities and limitations of causal reasoning in multi-agent frameworks, and how the human-in-the-loop can contribute to accurate and trustworthy results.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CausalSTeward (CAST), a human-in-the-loop framework for assembling large causal models using a divide-and-conquer approach and integrating prior knowledge with data-driven tools.
AI.motivation: Learning causal models from high-dimensional data is challenging, especially when real-world scenarios deviate from core assumptions, creating issues in causal identifiability. Effective integration of prior knowledge into causal discovery is still unsolved.
AI.method: CausalSTeward employs a multi-agent collaborative system that iteratively partitions large clusters of variables for separate analysis. It incorporates prior knowledge with data-driven tools, such as retrieval-augmented generation and conditional independence tests.
AI.result: The paper demonstrates that CAST can improve causal reasoning accuracy and contribute trustworthy results by effectively combining human input and computational methods.
AI.conclusion: CAST highlights the potential for enhancing causal discovery through a human-in-the-loop and multi-agent approach, addressing the high-dimensional complexity and limitations in current methods.

title: Bringing Agentic Search to Earth Observation Data Discovery
summary: NASA and its data centers hold thousands of geoscience datasets and tools like Worldview, Giovanni, the Science Discovery Engine, and Harmony. Finding the right one is hard even for domain experts. We present an agentic search system, deployed as a public service for the geoscience community, that takes a natural-language research query and returns the matching datasets and tools. We demonstrate that, in the era of large language models, the latent value of knowledge graphs (KGs) can be substantially amplified through agentic search. From the NASA Earth Observation Knowledge Graph (NASA EO-KG) we derive NASA-EO-Bench, an open benchmark of 47k query-dataset pairs (21k task-based queries). A neural scorer fine-tuned on NASA-EO-Bench beats cosine and BM25 baselines. Further combining it with BM25 via score fusion raises both Recall@10 (R@10) and MRR by over 5x. On top of this supervised pipeline, we add a zero-shot agentic reranking stage that, without any additional training, lifts MRR by 28% on a stratified N=200 subset, showing that LLM reasoning is complementary to supervised retrieval.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an AI-based search system that simplifies finding NASA geoscience datasets by combining knowledge graphs, neural scoring models, and large language models (LLMs).
AI.motivation: The challenge of navigating NASA's extensive database of geoscience datasets and tools highlights the need for a more effective search system to assist even domain experts.
AI.method: The method involves developing an agentic search system that processes natural-language queries. It employs NASA Earth Observation Knowledge Graph (EO-KG) to build a benchmark (NASA-EO-Bench), a fine-tuned neural scorer, BM25, and LLM-based reranking to enhance search performance.
AI.result: The neural scorer outperforms traditional retrieval methods, and combining it with BM25 significantly improves metrics like Recall@10 and MRR. Adding an LLM-based reranking phase further boosts performance by 28% in a subset.
AI.conclusion: Integrating knowledge graphs, neural scoring, and zero-shot LLM capabilities creates a more effective and complementary system for indexing and retrieving geoscience data, improving user accessibility.

title: Q-GAIN: A Python Package for Machine Learning and Physically Informed Analysis Applications
comment: Submission to SciPost, 20 pages with 4 figures
summary: Here we describe the quantum gas analysis and inference (Q-GAIN) Python package, which enables rapid deployment of machine learning (ML) and physics-informed analysis techniques for cold-atom experiments. Out of the box, Q-GAIN implements classification, object detection, and physics-informed metrics for feature detection in images of atomic Bose-Einstein condensates (BECs). Q-GAIN encourages a natural, module-based workflow: starting with data loading and preprocessing, followed by ML-based feature identification, and ending with conventional analysis techniques. We demonstrate this modularity by configuring Q-GAIN for three ML tasks. First, we demonstrate the basic workflow of the Q-GAIN framework by implementing the standard task of classifying handwritten digits from the MNIST dataset. Then, we re-implement our earlier soliton detection (SolDet) package in the Q-GAIN framework, enabling the detection and analysis of solitonic excitations in time-of-flight data. Finally, we develop an object-detection tool that identifies quantized vortices in images of ring-shaped BECs.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Q-GAIN, a Python package for applying machine learning and physics-based analysis techniques to cold-atom experiments, particularly Bose-Einstein condensates (BECs).
AI.motivation: To streamline and enhance the analysis of cold-atom experiment data by integrating machine learning and physics-informed methods into a user-friendly package.
AI.method: Developed the Q-GAIN Python package, which provides a modular workflow consisting of data loading, ML-based feature identification, and analysis. It supports classification, object detection, and physics-informed metrics, applied to tasks like digit classification (MNIST), soliton detection, and vortex identification in BECs.
AI.result: Q-GAIN successfully performs its intended tasks, including re-implementing existing soliton detection, demonstrating digit classification, and adding a new vortex object-detection capability for BEC data.
AI.conclusion: Q-GAIN simplifies and accelerates the analysis of cold-atom experiments through its modular, ML-enabled approach, making it a versatile tool for researchers in this domain.

title: A Multi-Branch Hierarchy-Aware Framework for Heterogeneous Audio Classification
summary: This technical report describes our system for Task 1 of the DCASE 2026 Challenge, which aims to classify heterogeneous audio recordings according to the Broad Sound Taxonomy (BST). The task requires both accurate second-level prediction and consistency with the top-level taxonomy. Our system is built on CLAP-based audio-text representations and is improved along three strategies: expanding the training set with a filtered subset of BSD35k, enhancing acoustic modeling with feature-specific branches, and refining predictions using hierarchy-aware classifiers and KNN-based post-processing. Among the acoustic features considered, the log-STFT branch provides the strongest single-model performance. With KNN-based post-processing, our best single system achieves a hierarchical F1 score (Hier. F1) of 80.84% on the BSD10k-v1.2 set under the same evaluation protocol as the baseline. We further construct ensemble systems by combining models with complementary acoustic features and classification heads, achieving Hier. F1 scores of 81.25% and 81.18%, respectively.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a method for classifying audio recordings according to the Broad Sound Taxonomy (BST) in the DCASE 2026 Challenge, achieving robust performance using innovative techniques.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to tackle the challenge of accurately classifying heterogeneous audio recordings while ensuring consistency with the hierarchical BST in a competitive setting.
AI.method: The system leverages CLAP-based audio-text representations and is enhanced by expanding the dataset, adding feature-specific branches for better acoustic modeling, and incorporating hierarchy-aware classifiers and KNN-based post-processing.
AI.result: The best single system achieved a hierarchical F1 score of 80.84%, while ensemble models achieved higher scores of 81.25% and 81.18% on BSD10k-v1.2, outperforming baseline results.
AI.conclusion: The proposed system demonstrates high accuracy and robustness in hierarchical audio classification, showing the efficacy of the applied strategies such as feature branching, hierarchical-aware techniques, and KNN post-processing.

title: Optimal Stabilizer Testing and Learning with Limited Quantum Memory
summary:
We study stabilizer state testing and learning with limited coherent quantum memory. Here an algorithm sequentially receives copies of an unknown $n$-qubit state, but may keep only $k$ qubits of coherent quantum memory between measurements. With unrestricted memory, seminal work of Gross, Nezami and Walter showed how to test $n$-qubit stabilizer states using $6$ copies, which is dimension independent, unlike the learning complexity of $Θ(n)$. We show that this testing-vs-learning separation is lost under memory constraints. More concretely we show that
  (1) The sample complexity of testing stabilizer states in the $k$-qubit memory framework is $Θ(n-k)$. Our upper bound goes via a novel connection to the hidden shift problem and the lower bound is proven using a novel approach to average case bounds on likelihood ratios via combinatorics of the stochastic orthogonal group.
  (2) The sample complexity of learning stabilizer states with $k$ qubits of memory, in the non-adaptive framework, is $Θ(n^2/k)$.
  As a further application of our techniques, we prove an exponential lower bound for purity testing even when the memory may be left coherent throughout the protocol. Our main results identify coherent quantum memory as the resource enabling the usual separation between stabilizer testing and learning. In particular, even with $k=0.99n$ qubits of memory, there is no constant-copy stabilizer tester; furthermore for $k=cn$ qubits of memory (for $0< c < 1$), stabilizer testing is as hard as learning, with both requiring $Θ(n)$ copies.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes the complexities of testing and learning stabilizer states under limited coherent quantum memory, discovering an interplay between memory limitations and sample requirements.
AI.motivation: To understand how constrained coherent memory impacts the ability to test and learn stabilizer states, addressing challenges in quantum information processing with limited resources.
AI.method: Developing novel techniques involving connections to hidden shift problems, likelihood ratios' combinatorics, and leveraging stochastic orthogonal groups.
AI.result: Stabilizer state testing with limited memory requires $Θ(n-k)$ samples, learning stabilizer states has a complexity of $Θ(n^2/k)$ samples in non-adaptive settings, and purity testing requires exponential samples even with coherent memory.
AI.conclusion: Coherent quantum memory is the pivotal resource for the separation between testing and learning stabilizer states. With constrained memory, both testing and learning become equally demanding, undermining typical separations.

title: An Efficient vLLM-Based Inference Pipeline for Unified Audio Understanding and Generation
summary: While Large Multimodal Models excel in comprehension, high-throughput inference engines lack native support for multimodal generation. This is severe in Speech Language Models, where generating multi-layered audio tokens via decoupled AR+NAR or synchronous Multi-Token Prediction (MTP) with delay-pattern interleaving conflicts with standard single-stream loops. We present a vLLM-based inference pipeline for unified speech understanding and generation. We extend autoregressive decoding to natively execute delay-pattern de-interleaving and coordinated multi-stream sampling, integrating an on-GPU acoustic decoder for end-to-end waveform synthesis. Crucially, we overcome the shared intuition that Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) halves throughput. By co-scheduling paired conditional and unconditional requests within a continuous batch, our CFG implementation sustains 80% of non-CFG throughput, absorbing dual-request and logit merging overheads. We open-source our framework.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a novel inference pipeline for speech models to address challenges in multimodal generation, focusing on efficient decoding and waveform synthesis.
AI.motivation: The research targets the inefficiencies in existing large multimodal models, especially for Speech Language Models, where traditional inference struggles with multimodal generation due to architectural mismatches with single-stream computational loops.
AI.method: The authors propose a vLLM-based pipeline featuring autoregressive decoding with delay-pattern de-interleaving and coordinated multi-stream sampling, and an on-GPU acoustic decoder for end-to-end audio generation.
AI.result: The proposed pipeline demonstrates efficient application of Classifier-Free Guidance while retaining 80% throughput compared to non-CFG methods, overcoming traditional performance limitations.
AI.conclusion: By addressing throughput and architectural inefficiencies, this work advances speech generation capabilities of multimodal models and provides an open-source framework for further development.

title: Behind the Refusal: Determining Guardrail Activation via Behavioral Monitoring
summary: As Large Language Models (LLMs) and agentic systems become integrated into real-world applications, ensuring their safety and security is critical. Guardrail systems that detect and block malicious instructions sent to and from an LLM are an essential component of AI security. However, researchers conducting black-box adversarial emulation against production AI systems often struggle to determine whether a guardrail block or an LLM rejection has occurred. This distinction is important because the techniques used to bypass guardrails can differ substantially from those used to bypass LLM safety alignment, and has a material impact on attack technique selection and optimization. We propose the first black-box guardrail reconnaissance methodology, which detects the presence of a guardrail within a target AI system through behavioral monitoring of HTTP, lexical, and timing signals, assuming only black-box access and zero prior knowledge of the guardrail or AI system. Experiments demonstrate that our approach detects guardrail presence with 100% accuracy, with statistically significant behavioral separation between benign and malicious interactions (q < 0.001). Our approach further identifies the content categories a guardrail is designed to block, and distinguishes guardrail blocks from LLM rejection on unseen prompts with an average F1 score of 98%.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a new method to identify guardrails in AI systems with high accuracy using black-box testing.
AI.motivation: Ensure safety and security of LLMs by detecting and analyzing their guardrails, which distinguish between malicious and safe instructions.
AI.method: Propose a black-box reconnaissance methodology based on monitoring HTTP, lexical, and timing signals to detect and study AI guardrails.
AI.result: The approach achieved 100% accuracy in detecting guardrails, and 98% F1 score in distinguishing between guardrail blocks and LLM rejections.
AI.conclusion: The method effectively identifies and analyzes guardrails, aiding in improved attack technique selection and AI system safety evaluation.

title: Overview of Risk Assessment and Management for Intelligent Systems under the AI Act and Beyond
comment: 6 pages, 1 figure, 1 table. Accepted at the IEEE International Carnahan Conference on Security Technology (ICCST 2026), October 14, 2026
summary: The society and emerging risk-based regulatory frameworks for AI underscore the need for rigorous risk assessment to ensure safe and reliable AI systems. In response to this imperative, this paper presents an overview of AI risk assessment (identification and analysis) and management methodologies. It begins by reviewing the worldwide regulatory landscape that drives the need for systematic AI risk assessment. Then we characterize the spectrum of AI-related risks identified in the literature, from technical failures to ethical and social impacts. Subsequently, it reviews key risk assessment methodologies proposed for AI systems, focusing on general frameworks. The paper highlights best practices and illuminates methodological gaps, highlighting areas for further research on AI risk assessment.

AI.tldr: This paper explores AI risk assessment and management, detailing global regulations, key risks, and methodologies while identifying gaps and best practices.
AI.motivation: Society and evolving AI regulatory frameworks demand effective risk assessments for safe AI systems.
AI.method: The paper reviews global regulatory landscapes, identifies various AI-related risks, and evaluates risk assessment methodologies, emphasizing general frameworks.
AI.result: Highlights best practices and identifies methodological gaps in AI risk assessments, suggesting areas for further research.
AI.conclusion: While existing methodologies provide insights, gaps remain in systematic AI risk assessments, underscoring the need for ongoing research.

title: What Types of Human-AI Teams Exist?
summary: Human-AI teaming has received increasing attention in the literature. However, the range of studies conducted in multiple domains make it difficult to understand what types of teams are being studied, and in what ways are they similar/different from one another. In this study, we analyse 53 papers on human-AI teams and categorise them into five main clusters based on psychological taxonomies of teaming; AI Assistant, Ad-hoc Dependency, Ad-hoc Forced Dependency, Paired Equanimity, and Group Equanimity. Each cluster represents a unique combination of holistic team-level characteristics, indicating there are multiple disparate team types studied under the same definition. In turn, this raises the question of whether insights are truly transferable between papers. We conclude with guidance on how to identify the types of human-AI teams studied, a checklist for reporting a human-AI team in research work, and ways in which the field can be further synthesised.

AI.tldr: The paper categorizes 53 studies on human-AI teams into five clusters based on teaming characteristics and raises concerns about the transferability of insights due to diverse definitions.
AI.motivation: To address the fragmented nature of studies on human-AI teams and understand the disparities between different types of teams studied under the same umbrella term.
AI.method: Analyzed 53 papers and classified them into five clusters using psychological taxonomies of teaming: AI Assistant, Ad-hoc Dependency, Ad-hoc Forced Dependency, Paired Equanimity, and Group Equanimity.
AI.result: Identified disparate types of human-AI teaming studied in the literature, highlighting the lack of uniformity in definitions and transferable insights.
AI.conclusion: Provides guidance on identifying and reporting human-AI team types and offers recommendations for synthesizing the research field.

title: The Eticas AI Risk Taxonomy: Open Infrastructure for Operationalizing AI Audits
summary: The rapid deployment of AI systems across high-stakes domains has created urgent demand for standardized evaluation, yet the field remains fragmented across competing risk taxonomies that catalog risks without showing how an audit is executed. At least 74 AI risk taxonomies exist, and almost all stop at the catalog. The hard part of auditing is not naming a risk but operationalizing it: turning it into a test run against a real system, a measured value, a calibrated severity, and a defensible grade. This paper leads with that bridge. We present the operationalization layer Eticas has built and run, shown end to end on a single risk (PII leakage) against a public benchmark, and then the open taxonomy that makes the method scale. On GPT-4-0314, a disclosure risk that seven external frameworks require be controlled is measured at 0%, 51%, and 84% disclosure as adversarial conditioning increases, mapping through calibrated severity bands to a subcategory grade of E with a SYSTEMIC pattern. Around this example, the Eticas AI Risk Taxonomy v2.0.0 organizes 76 active subcategories across 10 categories and 20 sub-groups, with mappings to 18 external frameworks across compliance, reference, and academic tiers. Its category and sub-group layer is published under CC BY 4.0 as open semantic infrastructure with stable URIs and SKOS/JSON-LD distributions, and a worked subcategory example shows the operational layer down to its severity thresholds. The contribution is the demonstrated bridge from concept to graded finding, anchored by a clean separation of risks from the mechanisms by which they surface, and framed by an open-core model in which the conceptual scaffold is open and the methodology calibration is the practitioner layer. This is the infrastructure the AI auditing field needs: shared, open, and demonstrably operable.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the fragmented state of AI risk evaluation by presenting an operational framework for auditing AI risks.
AI.motivation: The deployment of AI systems in critical domains has highlighted the need for standardized evaluations, yet current frameworks only catalog risks without offering actionable methodologies.
AI.method: The authors propose an operationalization system using the Eticas AI Risk Taxonomy to measure risks like PII leakage, and demonstrate its application against GPT-4.
AI.result: The framework enabled precise quantification of disclosure risks, with severity gradings and mappings to compliance frameworks, encapsulated in a scalable taxonomy of 76 subcategories.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a bridge from risk identification to actionable auditing methods, offering an open-core infrastructure vital for AI risk evaluation.

title: SelectTSL: Prompt-Guided Selective Target Sound Localization in Complex Scenarios
summary: Humans can selectively attend to a target sound and estimate its direction in complex scenarios, whereas such selective localization remains challenging for current deep learning-based systems. Sound source localization (SSL) has achieved remarkable success with deep learning, yet most methods localize all active sources without selectivity. Conversely, target sound extraction (TSE) extracts sources using multimodal prompts but typically fails to preserve the multichannel spatial information required for accurate localization. To bridge this gap, we formulate the task of prompt-guided selective target sound localization and propose SelectTSL, an end-to-end architecture that localizes only the user-specified target in multi-source acoustic scenes. Specifically, we design a target-aware selective localization strategy that employs a Prompt-Guided Selective Attention Module (PGSA) to generate prompt-informed embeddings. These embeddings guide an inter-channel phase difference (IPD) enhancer to refine raw phase cues, fusing with target magnitudes to jointly estimate direction of arrival (DoA) and target-source cardinality, i.e., the number of target sound sources. This coupled design effectively focuses on the user-specified target spatial cues for selective localization and also handles time-varying numbers of target sources. Extensive experiments on both synthetic data and real-world recordings demonstrate that our proposed method consistently outperforms other baselines and exhibits robust generalization to real acoustic environments.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces SelectTSL, an AI architecture for selectively localizing target sounds in noisy environments using a novel strategy involving prompt-informed embeddings and phase cue enhancement.
AI.motivation: The motivation lies in addressing the challenge of selective sound source localization in complex, multi-source environments, a task that humans excel at but current deep learning systems struggle with.
AI.method: The method introduces an end-to-end architecture, SelectTSL, utilizing a Prompt-Guided Selective Attention Module (PGSA). It refines raw phase cues through an inter-channel phase difference enhancer and fuses them with target magnitudes for accurate direction-of-arrival (DoA) estimation and source counting.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate that SelectTSL outperforms other baselines and generalizes well to real-world acoustic environments.
AI.conclusion: SelectTSL successfully bridges the gap between sound source localization and target extraction by enabling selective localization of user-specified sounds in real-world, multi-source scenarios.

title: Human Capital, Not Model Benchmarks, Predicts Hybrid Intelligence in Forecasting
summary: Whether pairing people with AI helps or hurts is usually reported as a single average effect. Using a real-money prediction market (Polymarket) as an objective, externally resolved benchmark, this pilot shows that the value of human-AI collaboration depends on a specific, measurable form of human capital. Analyzed at the level of the individual forecaster, hybrid performance is trimodal: most people either deferred to the model (matching it) or used it to rubber-stamp a prior guess (performing worse than the model alone), while a minority engaged in genuine complementary reasoning and reached accuracy matching or even exceeding (i.e., lower error than) the market itself. Collaborative traits (perspective-taking, intellectual humility, and curiosity) rather than raw cognitive ability or model benchmarks, distinguished who reached that mode. The results are preliminary but statistically robust, and motivate a pre-registered replication now in preparation.

AI.tldr: This paper examines how human-AI collaboration varies by individual characteristics, using a prediction market as the metric of success.
AI.motivation: To determine what factors influence the effectiveness of human-AI collaboration and whether certain human traits enable better outcomes.
AI.method: A pilot study using real-money prediction markets as a benchmark to measure individual forecasters' hybrid performance with an AI model.
AI.result: Hybrid performance was trimodal: most either matched or underperformed the AI, while a minority outperformed it when showing collaborative traits like perspective-taking, humility, and curiosity.
AI.conclusion: Human-AI collaboration effectiveness depends on measurable psychological traits, suggesting tailored intervention could enhance outcomes; a replication study is planned.
